1:
"And the whole multitude of them arose, and led him unto Pilate." The witnesses note the delivering of Christ to the Roman governor and its prophetic fulfilment. Augustine observes the narrative order: Luke, "after relating the denial of Peter, recapitulated all that took place... and so he has composed his narrative." Bede marks the fulfilment of prophecy: "that the word of Jesus might be fulfilled which He prophesied of His own death, He shall be delivered to the Gentiles, that is, to the Romans; for Pilate was a Roman, sent as governor to Judea." The witnesses note the whole council leading Christ from their own court to Pilate's judgment-hall.

The witnesses note Christ delivered to the Gentile governor — the Sanhedrin, lacking power to execute, handing him to Rome.

So the witnesses make the verse the delivering of Christ to Pilate. The lesson the expositors draw is the fulfilment of prophecy in Christ's being handed over to the Gentiles, and the transition from the religious to the civil trial. The witnesses note that "the whole multitude of them arose, and led him unto Pilate" — the council, having condemned Christ as a blasphemer (Luke 22:71), now leads him to the Roman governor; for the Jews, under Roman rule, lacked the power to execute, and must obtain Pilate's sentence to put Christ to death (John 18:31). The expositors press the prophetic fulfilment Bede marks: Christ had foretold that he would be "delivered to the Gentiles" (Luke 18:32; Mark 10:33); and now the word is fulfilled, the Sanhedrin handing him to Pilate the Roman — the Messiah of Israel delivered by his own people into the hands of the heathen. The witnesses observe the deepening of the humiliation: condemned by the rulers of his own nation, Christ is now arraigned before the Gentile power, that both Jew and Gentile might have a hand in his death — and that the prophecy of his being delivered to the nations might be accomplished. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence overruling the trial: the very malice of the rulers, in handing Christ to Pilate, served to fulfil the Scriptures and to accomplish the appointed manner of his death — crucifixion, the Roman penalty, the lifting up he had foretold (John 12:32-33). The witnesses note Augustine's observation on Luke's careful narrative: the evangelist, having recounted Peter's denial, recapitulates the morning's events, composing his account in harmony with the others, each supplying what the others omit. The verse thus records the delivering of Christ to Pilate — the council handing him to the Gentile governor — teaching the fulfilment of prophecy in his being delivered to the Gentiles, the transition to the civil trial, and the providence overruling the malice of men to accomplish the appointed death of the Saviour.
2:
"And they began to accuse him, saying, We found this fellow perverting the nation, and forbidding to give tribute to Caesar, saying that he himself is Christ a King." The witnesses note the false political charges and a rabbinic background. Augustine observes that "Luke has laid open the very charges which they falsely brought against Him." Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic phrase behind "perverting the nation": "to corrupt their food publicly" was a Talmudic expression "to denote a mingling of true doctrine with heresy" — the charge being that Christ taught principles tending "to turn off the people from their obedience to the Romans." Barnes notes "this fellow" conveys "a notion of contempt." The witnesses note the threefold charge — sedition, forbidding tribute, claiming kingship — framed to make Christ appear a rebel against Caesar.

The witnesses note the false political charges: perverting the nation, forbidding tribute, claiming to be a king — calculated to alarm Rome.

So the witnesses make the verse the false accusation before Pilate. The lesson the expositors draw is the malicious craft of the accusers, who, abandoning the charge of blasphemy that moved them, framed political charges calculated to compel the Roman governor. The witnesses note the shift in the accusation: before the Sanhedrin, Christ was condemned for blasphemy, for claiming to be the Son of God; but before Pilate, the Romans caring nothing for Jewish religious disputes, the accusers frame charges that would alarm Rome — "perverting the nation," stirring sedition; "forbidding to give tribute to Caesar," inciting rebellion; "saying that he himself is Christ a King," claiming a kingship that rivalled Caesar's. The expositors press the falsehood of these charges Augustine marks: Luke records the very accusations, which were false — Christ had not perverted the nation, but taught the truth; he had not forbidden tribute, but expressly commanded it ("render unto Caesar," Luke 20:25); and his kingship, which he owned, was not of this world (John 18:36), no rival to Caesar. The witnesses observe Lightfoot's note on the rabbinic phrase behind "perverting the nation" — the charge of mingling heresy with truth, turning the people from their obedience; a calculated slander. The expositors draw the lesson on the craft of malice: the accusers, knowing the blasphemy-charge would not move Pilate, twisted Christ's words and deeds into political crimes — making the One who taught obedience to Caesar appear a rebel against him. The witnesses note the contempt in "this fellow" (Barnes), the scorn with which they arraigned the Lord of glory. The expositors observe the irony: they charged Christ with forbidding tribute, when he had commanded it; with claiming a rival kingship, when his kingdom was not of this world — the very opposite of the truth. The verse thus records the false political accusation before Pilate — sedition, tribute, kingship — teaching the malicious craft of the accusers, the falsehood of the charges, and the twisting of Christ's truth into the appearance of treason.
3:
"And Pilate asked him, saying, Art thou the King of the Jews? And he answered him and said, Thou sayest it." The witnesses note Pilate's question and Christ's confession. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "all the Gospels give Pilate's question in precisely the same words" (Mark 15:2; Matthew 27:11; John 18:33). Ellicott marks that "Thou sayest it" is "a formula of confession" (as in Luke 22:70), and that "the fuller narrative of St. John should be compared." Gill notes Pilate seized on the kingship charge, ignoring the religious matter. The witnesses note Christ affirming, before Pilate, that he is the King of the Jews — yet (as John shows) a king whose kingdom is not of this world.

The witnesses note Christ's confession of his kingship before Pilate — owning the title, yet of a kingdom not of this world.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's confession of his kingship. The lesson the expositors draw is the open avowal of Christ's kingship before the Roman governor, joined (in the fuller account) to the truth that his kingdom is not of this world. The witnesses note Pilate's question, fastening on the political charge: "Art thou the King of the Jews?" — for of the three charges, only the claim of kingship concerned the Roman governor, who cared nothing for the religious dispute. And Christ answers, "Thou sayest it" — a formula of confession (Ellicott), an affirmative: yes, I am the King of the Jews. The expositors press the fuller truth that John supplies (John 18:33-37): Christ confessed his kingship, but explained that his kingdom is "not of this world" — no rival to Caesar, no earthly dominion won by the sword, but a spiritual reign of truth; so that his confession, true as it was, gave Pilate no ground to condemn him as a rebel. The witnesses observe the boldness of the confession: Christ does not deny his kingship to escape, but openly owns it — King of the Jews, the Messiah promised to the fathers — though he knows the title would be twisted against him. The expositors draw the lesson: Christ is indeed a King, the King of his people, but his kingdom is not of this world; he reigns by truth and grace, not by earthly power; and his confession before Pilate is the avowal of a kingship that no earthly throne can rival, for it is spiritual and eternal. The witnesses note the harmony of the Gospels Robertson marks: all four give Pilate's question identically — a token of the firm tradition of this central exchange. The expositors observe the dignity of Christ in his confession: bound and accused, he yet calmly owns his royal title, the true King standing before the earthly governor. The verse thus records Christ's confession of his kingship before Pilate — "Thou sayest it" — teaching the open avowal of his royal title, the truth (in John) that his kingdom is not of this world, and the dignity of the true King before the earthly judge.
4:
"Then said Pilate to the chief priests and to the people, I find no fault in this man." The witnesses note Pilate's first declaration of Christ's innocence. The Reformed Gill notes this was Pilate's "second time of coming out to them," after examining Jesus privately. Barnes explains the ground: "after Pilate had taken Jesus into the judgment-hall and examined him privately, he was satisfied that though he claimed to be a king, yet his kingdom was not of this world, and his claims did not interfere with those of Caesar" (John 18:33-38). The witnesses note Pilate, having examined Christ, declaring him innocent of the charges — the first of his repeated acquittals.

The witnesses note Pilate's verdict of innocence: "I find no fault in this man."

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's first declaration of Christ's innocence. The lesson the expositors draw is the testimony of the Roman judge himself to Christ's innocence, the heathen governor acquitting the One the rulers condemned. The witnesses note that Pilate, having privately examined Christ (John 18:33-38) and understood that his kingdom was not of this world and posed no threat to Caesar, came out to the accusers and declared, "I find no fault in this man" — no guilt, no ground for the charges; the first of his repeated acquittals. The expositors press the weight of this testimony: it was the Roman governor, the judge appointed to try the case, who pronounced Christ innocent — a heathen, with no love for the Jews, yet compelled by the evidence to acquit the prisoner; the very judge bearing witness to the guiltlessness of the Lamb. The witnesses observe the fulfilment of prophecy and the truth it confirms: Christ was indeed innocent, holy, harmless, undefiled (Hebrews 7:26; 1 Peter 2:22); and his innocence was attested not by friends only but by his Gentile judge, that all might know he died not as a malefactor but as the spotless sacrifice. The expositors draw the lesson: the repeated declarations of Pilate (this is the first of several) establish beyond doubt that Christ was guiltless of any crime — condemned, in the end, not by justice but by the clamour of the mob and the cowardice of the judge; his death the murder of the innocent, the offering of the sinless for sinners. The witnesses note the contrast: the rulers, who should have received their Messiah, condemned him; the heathen judge, who had no claim on him, acquitted him — a rebuke to the unbelief of the nation. The expositors observe the providence: even Pilate's acquittal served to display Christ's innocence, that his death might be seen as a sacrifice, not a punishment. The verse thus records Pilate's first declaration of Christ's innocence — "I find no fault" — teaching the testimony of the Gentile judge to the guiltlessness of Christ, the establishing of his innocence, and the truth that he died not as a criminal but as the spotless sacrifice for sin.
5:
"And they were the more fierce, saying, He stirreth up the people, teaching throughout all Jewry, beginning from Galilee to this place." The witnesses note the accusers' increased fury. The Reformed Gill notes "they were the more urgent... more clamorous and noisy." Barnes explains the tactic: "they saw that there was a prospect of losing their cause, and attempted to press the point most likely to affect Pilate... Pilate had acquitted him of being an enemy to Caesar, and they therefore urged the other point" — that he stirred up the people throughout the land, from Galilee to Jerusalem. The witnesses note the accusers, seeing Pilate inclined to acquit, pressing the charge of sedition more vehemently.

The witnesses note the accusers' rising fury: insisting Christ stirred up the people throughout the land.

So the witnesses make the verse the accusers' renewed and fiercer charge. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of malice, which presses harder as its cause seems lost, and the providential mention of Galilee that would send Christ to Herod. The witnesses note that the accusers, far from yielding to Pilate's acquittal, "were the more fierce" — more clamorous, more vehement; seeing the prospect of losing their cause, they pressed the charge most likely to move the governor. The expositors observe the tactic Barnes marks: Pilate had acquitted Christ of being a rival to Caesar, so the accusers urged the other point — that he "stirreth up the people," exciting sedition and tumult, "throughout all Jewry, beginning from Galilee to this place"; a charge of widespread agitation, calculated to alarm the Roman, who feared above all the unrest of the province. The witnesses press the persistence of their malice: defeated in the verdict, they do not relent but redouble their fury, determined to compass Christ's death by whatever charge would serve. The expositors draw the lesson on the relentlessness of unbelief and hatred: the accusers, resolved on Christ's death, would not be turned by his acquittal, but pressed on with rising vehemence — a warning of the implacable enmity of the human heart against Christ when it is hardened. The witnesses note the providential mention of "Galilee," which (the next verses show) gave Pilate his pretext to send Christ to Herod, the tetrarch of Galilee — the accusers' charge unwittingly serving the appointed course of the trial. The expositors observe the falsehood of the charge: Christ had not stirred up the people to sedition, but taught the way of peace and obedience; his "stirring" was the stirring of hearts to repentance and faith, not to rebellion. The witnesses draw the application: the malice that pursued Christ pursues his cause still, pressing harder as it seems to lose; and the believer is not to be surprised at the persistence of opposition. The verse thus records the accusers' renewed and fiercer charge — that Christ stirred up the people from Galilee to Jerusalem — teaching the persistence of malice, the false charge of sedition, and the providential mention of Galilee that would send Christ to Herod.
6:
"When Pilate heard of Galilee, he asked whether the man were a Galilaean." The witnesses note Pilate's eagerness to escape judgment. Bede marks the motive: "Pilate, having determined not to question our Lord concerning the accusation, is the rather glad now that an opportunity offers to escape from passing judgment upon Him... lest he should be compelled to pass sentence against one whom he knew to be innocent, and delivered for envy, [he] sends Him to be heard by Herod." Theophylact notes this followed the Roman law, "which provided that every man should be judged by the governor of his own jurisdiction." The witnesses note Pilate seizing on the mention of Galilee as a way to be rid of the case.

The witnesses note Pilate's relief at the mention of Galilee — a way to escape judging the innocent.

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's attempt to escape judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is the cowardice of Pilate, who, knowing Christ innocent, sought to avoid the duty of acquitting him. The witnesses note Pilate's question, prompted by the mention of Galilee: "whether the man were a Galilaean" — seizing on the geographical detail as a pretext to transfer the case. The expositors press Bede's reading of the motive: Pilate, convinced of Christ's innocence yet unwilling to brave the rulers' anger by releasing him, was glad of an excuse to be rid of the troublesome case — to send Christ to another judge, that he himself might escape both the guilt of condemning the innocent and the danger of acquitting him against the rulers' will. The witnesses observe the cowardice this betrays: Pilate, the judge, ought to have acquitted Christ outright, having found no fault; but, fearing the people and shrinking from the conflict, he looked for a way to shift the responsibility, seizing on the jurisdiction of Herod. The expositors note the legal pretext Theophylact marks: by Roman law, a man might be judged by the governor of his own province; and Christ, being a Galilean, fell under Herod's jurisdiction — so Pilate had a lawful occasion to send him to Herod, the tetrarch of Galilee, then in Jerusalem for the feast. The witnesses draw the lesson on the moral weakness of Pilate: he knew the right (Christ's innocence) but lacked the courage to do it; and so, instead of acquitting, he temporized, seeking to evade the decision — the first step in the cowardice that would end in condemning the innocent to please the crowd. The expositors observe the warning: the judge who will not do justice for fear of men begins a descent that ends in injustice; Pilate's evasion is the prelude to his fall. The witnesses note the providence: Pilate's attempt to escape served to bring Christ before Herod, that Herod too might bear witness to his innocence, and the prophecy of the kings and rulers gathered against the Lord be fulfilled (Psalm 2:2; Acts 4:27). The verse thus records Pilate's seizing on Galilee to escape judgment — teaching the cowardice of the judge who knew the right but feared to do it, the legal pretext for sending Christ to Herod, and the providence overruling his evasion.
7:
"And as soon as he knew that he belonged unto Herod's jurisdiction, he sent him to Herod, who himself also was at Jerusalem at that time." The witnesses note the sending to Herod and its motives. The Reformed Gill identifies "Herod Antipas, the son of Herod the great, and the same that beheaded John the Baptist," tetrarch of Galilee; and gives Pilate's motives: "partly that he might be rid of this troublesome business; and partly that he might show to Herod what a regard he had to his jurisdiction." Lightfoot discusses whether Pilate yielded Herod a jurisdiction in capital matters, suggesting it was done "either in flattery" or to honour Herod. The witnesses note Pilate sending Christ to Herod, who was in Jerusalem for the feast.

The witnesses note Christ sent to Herod — Pilate ridding himself of the case and honouring the tetrarch.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of Christ to Herod. The lesson the expositors draw is Pilate's double motive of evasion and policy, and the providence that brought Christ before yet another judge. The witnesses note that Pilate, learning Christ belonged to Herod's jurisdiction (Galilee), "sent him to Herod" — the tetrarch of Galilee, who happened to be in Jerusalem for the Passover. The expositors observe the motives Gill names: Pilate sent Christ partly to be rid of the troublesome case, escaping the duty of judging the innocent; and partly as a courtesy to Herod, showing respect for his jurisdiction, and so (as the next verses show) reconciling the long-standing enmity between them. The witnesses press the cowardice and policy mingled in the act: Pilate would not acquit Christ outright, fearing the rulers; nor condemn him, knowing him innocent; so he shifted the case to Herod, hoping to evade the decision and to gratify the tetrarch. The expositors note Herod's identity: this was Herod Antipas, the murderer of John the Baptist, who had long wished to see Jesus; so the providence that sent Christ to him answered Herod's curiosity, and brought the Lord before the very man who had slain his forerunner. The witnesses draw the lesson on the trial's course: Christ is passed from judge to judge — the Sanhedrin, Pilate, Herod, Pilate again — none willing to do justice, all entangling themselves in his blood; the innocent shuttled between cowardly and curious judges, that the guilt might be spread and the prophecy fulfilled. The expositors observe the fulfilment of Psalm 2: the kings of the earth (Herod) and the rulers (Pilate, the priests) gathered together against the Lord and his Anointed (Acts 4:26-27) — the providence overruling Pilate's evasion to gather all the powers against Christ, as foretold. The witnesses note the irony: Pilate's attempt to escape responsibility only widened the circle of those implicated in Christ's death. The verse thus records the sending of Christ to Herod — Pilate's evasion and courtesy — teaching the cowardice and policy of the judge, the providence bringing Christ before yet another tribunal, and the fulfilment of prophecy in the gathering of the rulers against the Lord.
8:
"And when Herod saw Jesus, he was exceeding glad: for he was desirous to see him of a long season, because he had heard many things of him; and he hoped to have seen some miracle done by him." The witnesses note Herod's curiosity and its base motive. Gregory notes "Herod wished to make proof of Christ's fame, desiring to witness His miracles." Theophylact marks the shallowness: "not as though he was about to gain any benefit from the sight, but seized with curiosity." Ellicott traces the history: "the vague feeling of wonder had begun soon after the death of the Baptist," ending "in a desire to see one," yet "mingled, possibly, with a feeling of bitter enmity which no miracle could remove." The witnesses note Herod glad to see Jesus, hoping for a miracle as a spectacle.

The witnesses note Herod's idle curiosity: glad to see Jesus, hoping for a miracle as entertainment.

So the witnesses make the verse Herod's curious gladness. The lesson the expositors draw is the shallow, idle curiosity of Herod, who desired Christ not as a Saviour but as a wonder-worker to gratify his curiosity. The witnesses note Herod's joy: "exceeding glad" to see Jesus, of whom he had long heard much (since John's death, Luke 9:9), and whom he had long wished to see. The expositors press the base motive: Herod desired not to learn from Christ, nor to receive him, but "hoped to have seen some miracle done by him" — to witness a wonder, as one craves a spectacle; his interest was idle curiosity, not faith. The witnesses observe the shallowness Theophylact marks: Herod expected no benefit from the sight, but was merely "seized with curiosity," wishing to see the famous wonder-worker perform — treating the Lord of glory as an entertainer who might amuse him with a miracle. The expositors draw the lesson on the wrong way of approaching Christ: Herod sought a sign, a marvel, a gratification of his curiosity — not salvation, not truth, not repentance; and such an approach, the witnesses note, finds no answer, for Christ does not work miracles to gratify idle curiosity. The expositors observe Ellicott's note on the mingled enmity: Herod's curiosity was joined, perhaps, with the bitter enmity of one who had slain John and feared Jesus might be John risen (Luke 9:7-9) — a curiosity that no miracle could turn to faith, for the heart was hardened. The witnesses press the contrast with true seeking: those who came to Christ in faith and need found him a Saviour; but Herod, coming in idle curiosity, found him silent (the next verse) — for Christ reveals himself to faith, not to the mere seeker of wonders. The expositors draw the application: men still approach Christ as Herod did — curious about him, desiring some sign or sensation, but not seeking him as Saviour; and such curiosity, however eager, is barren, finding no saving response. The verse thus records Herod's curious gladness — hoping for a miracle as a spectacle — teaching the shallowness of idle curiosity about Christ, the wrong way of seeking him for wonders rather than salvation, and the hardness that no miracle could cure.
9:
"Then he questioned with him in many words; but he answered him nothing." The witnesses note Christ's silence before Herod. The Reformed Gill notes Herod "talked very much... inquiring who he was," and "used many arguments to persuade him to work a miracle." Barnes connects the silence to prophecy (Psalm 38:13-14; 39:9; Isaiah 53:7). Ellicott reflects on the contrast: "the unbroken silence of the Accused must have been strangely impressive... when we remember how He had answered Caiaphas when adjured in the name of the living God" — Christ answering the high priest's solemn question, yet silent before Herod's idle one. The witnesses note Christ, questioned much, answering Herod nothing.

The witnesses note Christ's silence before Herod — answering the idle questioner not a word.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's silence before Herod. The lesson the expositors draw is the majestic silence of Christ before the idle and hardened Herod, fulfilling prophecy and rebuking curiosity. The witnesses note that Herod "questioned with him in many words" — talking much, asking who he was, pressing him to work a miracle; but Christ "answered him nothing" — not a word, an unbroken silence. The expositors press the meaning of the silence: Christ, who had confessed his deity before Caiaphas when solemnly adjured (Luke 22:70), and who answered Pilate's question, was silent before Herod — for Herod sought not truth but a spectacle, and Christ does not cast his pearls before such a one, nor work wonders to gratify idle curiosity. The witnesses observe Ellicott's striking contrast: the silence is the more impressive set against Christ's answer to Caiaphas; he spoke to the high priest's solemn question, but to Herod's idle words he gave no reply — a silence that judged Herod's frivolity and hardness. The expositors draw the lesson on the wrong approach to Christ: Herod, who sought a sign for his amusement, received nothing; for Christ reveals himself to faith and need, not to curiosity and unbelief. The witnesses note the fulfilment of prophecy Barnes marks: "as a sheep before her shearers is dumb, so he openeth not his mouth" (Isaiah 53:7); the silent Lamb, who answered not the idle questioner, fulfilling the Servant's silence under his accusers. The expositors observe the dignity and the judgment in the silence: Christ's silence was not weakness but majesty — the refusal of the Lord of glory to be made a spectacle, and a solemn judgment on Herod, who had slain John and now trifled with Jesus; the silence itself a sentence on the hardened heart. The witnesses draw the application: those who approach Christ in idle curiosity, seeking signs and sensations rather than salvation, meet his silence; the Lord speaks to the seeking soul, but is dumb before the trifler. The verse thus records Christ's silence before Herod — answering the idle questioner nothing — teaching the majestic silence of the Lamb, the fulfilment of prophecy, and the judgment of Christ's silence on idle curiosity and hardened unbelief.
10:
"And the chief priests and scribes stood and vehemently accused him." The witnesses note the accusers' relentless pursuit before Herod. The Reformed Gill notes the Sanhedrin "followed him from Pilate's hall to Herod's palace; fearing lest Herod should be disposed to let him go." Adam Clarke draws the lesson: "corrupt priests and teachers are generally the most implacable enemies of Christ and his truth... an affected moderation would have rendered their accusations more probable, and the envy less visible than this vehemence: but envy seldom consults prudence." The witnesses note the priests and scribes standing before Herod, vehemently accusing Christ.

The witnesses note the accusers pursuing Christ to Herod's court, accusing him vehemently.

So the witnesses make the verse the accusers' relentless pursuit. The lesson the expositors draw is the implacable malice of the priests and scribes, who pursued Christ from court to court with vehement accusation. The witnesses note that the chief priests and scribes "stood and vehemently accused him" — having followed Christ from Pilate's hall to Herod's palace, they continued their charges with fury, fearing (Gill notes) lest Herod, like Pilate, should incline to release him. The expositors press the relentlessness of their malice: not content with the Sanhedrin's condemnation and Pilate's hearing, they dogged Christ to Herod's court, redoubling their accusations, determined that no judge should let him go; their hatred would not rest until they had compassed his death. The witnesses observe Clarke's reflection on the vehemence: "envy seldom consults prudence" — the priests' very vehemence betrayed their envy and malice, where an affected moderation would have made their charges more credible; their fury exposed the falseness of their cause. The expositors draw the lesson on the enmity of corrupt religious leaders: "corrupt priests and teachers are generally the most implacable enemies of Christ" — those who should have led men to him are often his fiercest foes when their position and pride are threatened; the guardians of religion the persecutors of its Lord. The witnesses note the contrast with Christ's silence: the accusers cried out vehemently, but Christ answered nothing; the louder their fury, the more impressive his calm — and (as Theophylact noted on the trial) the very silence of Christ against the clamour of his enemies was a witness to his innocence. The expositors observe the providence: God permitted this relentless pursuit, that Christ's innocence might be the more displayed — for despite all their vehement accusation, neither Herod nor Pilate found any fault in him. The witnesses draw the application: the malice that pursued Christ pursues his cause and his people still, implacable and unresting; and the believer is not to be surprised when the enemies of the truth, especially the corrupt religious, pursue him with vehemence. The verse thus records the accusers' relentless pursuit before Herod — vehemently accusing Christ — teaching the implacable malice of the priests and scribes, the envy betrayed by their vehemence, and the enmity of corrupt religion against the Lord.
11:
"And Herod with his men of war set him at nought, and mocked him, and arrayed him in a gorgeous robe, and sent him again to Pilate." The witnesses note Herod's contempt and the mocking robe. Barnes explains the robe: "a white or shining robe... the Jewish kings wore a white robe, often rendered very gorgeous by tinsel or silver interwoven" — so "the Jews and Romans decked him in the manner appropriate to their own country, for purposes of mockery." Gill notes "his men of war" were Herod's bodyguard. The witnesses note Herod, baffled by Christ's silence, treating him with contempt, mocking him as a pretended king, and sending him back to Pilate.

The witnesses note Herod's scorn: mocking Christ as a mock-king in a gorgeous robe, then sending him back.

So the witnesses make the verse Herod's contemptuous mockery. The lesson the expositors draw is the scorn of Herod, who, baffled by Christ's silence, turned from curiosity to contempt, mocking the King of kings. The witnesses note that Herod "with his men of war set him at nought" — Herod and his soldiers treated Christ with contempt, despising him; the curiosity that had hoped for a miracle, disappointed by his silence, soured into scorn. The expositors press the mockery of his kingship: they "arrayed him in a gorgeous robe" — a shining, kingly garment (Barnes notes the white robe of Jewish royalty, made splendid with silver) — dressing him as a mock-king, to deride his claim to be the King of the Jews; the robe a jeer at his royalty. The witnesses observe the irony the expositors draw: Herod and his men mocked Christ as a pretended king, arraying him in royal robes in derision — yet he was the true King, the King of kings, whom they despised; their mockery, meant as scorn, unwittingly proclaimed the truth they denied. The expositors draw the lesson on the contempt of the worldly great: Herod, who had hoped to be entertained, finding no spectacle, despised and mocked the Lord of glory — the scorn of the proud for the One who would not gratify their idle curiosity. The witnesses note the patience of Christ under this mockery: silent before Herod's questions, he is silent under his scorn, enduring the contempt and the mocking robe without a word — the meek Lamb bearing the derision of his creatures. The expositors observe that Herod, baffled, sent Christ back to Pilate — neither acquitting nor condemning, but returning the case, his own contribution being only mockery; yet his sending back was, in effect, a confession that he found no crime, no ground for condemnation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer, mocked and despised for Christ, follows a Master who bore the scorn of Herod; and the world's contempt of Christ, like Herod's, often unwittingly testifies to the truth it means to deny. The verse thus records Herod's contemptuous mockery — the gorgeous robe, the scorn, the sending back — teaching the contempt of the worldly great for Christ, the patience of the Lamb under derision, and the irony of mocking as a false king the true King of kings.
12:
"And the same day Pilate and Herod were made friends together: for before they were at enmity between themselves." The witnesses note the reconciliation of the two judges over Christ. The Reformed Gill explains the cause: Herod was pleased that Pilate "should show such a regard to his authority as to send one that belonged to his jurisdiction," and Pilate that Herod referred the case back to him. Barnes notes "what had been the cause of their quarrel is unknown" (perhaps the Galileans Pilate slew, Luke 13:1), and observes — against the common notion — that "it was not because they were united in hating Jesus, as is often the case with wicked men, for Pilate was certainly desirous" of releasing him. The witnesses note the former enemies reconciled through the courtesy shown in Christ's trial.

The witnesses note the reconciliation of Pilate and Herod — former enemies made friends over the trial of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the reconciling of the two judges. The lesson the expositors draw is the strange friendship born over Christ's trial, and the providence and irony in former enemies united. The witnesses note that "Pilate and Herod were made friends together" that very day, "for before they were at enmity" — the two rulers, formerly at odds (the cause unknown, perhaps Pilate's slaying of Herod's Galileans, Luke 13:1), now reconciled. The expositors observe the occasion Gill names: the mutual courtesy shown in Christ's trial — Pilate honouring Herod's jurisdiction by sending Christ to him, Herod honouring Pilate by referring the case back; the exchange of deference reconciling their quarrel. The witnesses note the careful distinction Barnes draws, against the common reading: it was not simply that they were "united in hating Jesus" (as wicked men often unite against a common foe), for Pilate, in fact, desired to release Christ; rather, the reconciliation sprang from the mutual respect shown in the handling of the case. The expositors draw the lesson, weighing the readings: many find here the dark truth that enmity is often healed in a common opposition to Christ — Herod and Pilate, like Psalm 2's kings and rulers, gathered against the Lord (Acts 4:27); yet Barnes rightly notes that Pilate's case was not simple hatred, but cowardice, since he wished to free Christ. The witnesses observe the providence and the irony: that the trial of Christ, the occasion of so much sin, should also be the occasion of reconciling two enemies — the Prince of Peace, even in his condemnation, the cause (though they knew it not) of peace between his judges. The expositors note the solemn fulfilment of prophecy: Herod and Pilate, with the Gentiles and the people of Israel, gathered together against God's holy Servant (Acts 4:27) — their new friendship part of the alliance of the powers against Christ. The witnesses draw the application: men are too easily reconciled in their common treatment of Christ, the world finding a strange unity in its dealings with him; let the believer beware of friendships forged in opposition to the Lord. The verse thus records the reconciling of Pilate and Herod — former enemies made friends over Christ's trial — teaching the strange friendship born in his condemnation, the providence and irony of enemies united, and the fulfilment of prophecy in the gathering of the rulers against the Lord.
13:
"And Pilate, when he had called together the chief priests and the rulers and the people," The witnesses note Pilate's formal reconvening to give his verdict. Augustine notes Luke "returns to those things going on before the governor, from which he had digressed to relate what took place with Herod." Ambrose marks the tragic role of Pilate: "here Pilate, who as a judge acquits Christ, is made the minister of His crucifixion... they both refuse to pronounce Him guilty, yet for fear's sake, Pilate gratifies the cruel desires of the Jews." Theophylact sees in the two acquittals a witness: "by the testimony of two men, Jesus is declared innocent." The witnesses note Pilate gathering the accusers to render his judgment.

The witnesses note Pilate reconvening the council and people to deliver his verdict on Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's formal reconvening for the verdict. The lesson the expositors draw is the doubly-attested innocence of Christ and the tragic weakness of Pilate, who acquits yet condemns. The witnesses note that Pilate "called together the chief priests and the rulers and the people" — formally reconvening the accusers, now that Christ was sent back from Herod, to deliver his judgment; the official setting for the verdict. The expositors press the witness of the two judges that Theophylact marks: both Pilate and Herod, having examined Christ, found no fault in him; so by the testimony of two — the required number for valid witness (Deuteronomy 19:15) — Jesus is declared innocent, the truth doubly attested by his very judges. The witnesses observe the triumph of truth Theophylact notes: Christ is silent, yet his enemies' own judges witness for him; the Jews cry loudly, yet not one credible witness corroborates their charge — truth prevailing through the very proceedings meant to condemn him. The expositors draw the lesson on the tragedy of Pilate that Ambrose presses: Pilate, who as judge acquits Christ, is yet "made the minister of His crucifixion" — he refuses to pronounce him guilty, yet, for fear's sake, gratifies the cruel desires of the Jews; the judge who knows the right but lacks the courage to do it becomes the instrument of the very injustice he condemns. The witnesses note the fearful warning in this: to know the truth and yet, through fear of men, to act against it, is the path of Pilate — acquitting with the lips, condemning with the hands; a cowardice that makes the innocent's judge his executioner. The expositors observe the providence: even Pilate's reconvening, meant to find a way out, served to display Christ's innocence once more before the verdict that would send him to the cross. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned against the cowardice that betrays known truth for fear of men, and is to mark how Christ's innocence was attested even by his judges. The verse thus records Pilate's reconvening for the verdict — teaching the doubly-attested innocence of Christ, the triumph of truth through his very trial, and the tragic weakness of Pilate, who acquits yet is made the minister of the crucifixion.
14:
"Said unto them, Ye have brought this man unto me, as one that perverteth the people: and, behold, I, having examined him before you, have found no fault in this man touching those things whereof ye accuse him:" The witnesses note Pilate's renewed declaration of innocence. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Pilate "condenses the three charges of verse 2 into one" — that Christ is "a revolutionary agitator and dangerous rival to Caesar, treason in plain words" — and "having examined him before you" means "right before your eyes I have given him a careful examination." The witnesses note Pilate declaring that, after examining Christ in their presence, he found no fault touching their charges.

The witnesses note Pilate's public acquittal: after examination, no fault found touching the charges.

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's renewed public acquittal. The lesson the expositors draw is the formal, repeated testimony of the judge to Christ's innocence, leaving the rulers without excuse. The witnesses note Pilate's summary: "ye have brought this man unto me, as one that perverteth the people" — Robertson notes he condenses the three charges of verse 2 (perverting the nation, forbidding tribute, claiming kingship) into the one accusation of being a dangerous agitator, treason against Caesar. And Pilate declares: "having examined him before you, [I] have found no fault in this man touching those things whereof ye accuse him" — after a careful examination, conducted openly before the accusers, he found Christ guiltless of the charges. The expositors press the weight of this formal acquittal: it was a public, judicial declaration, made before the accusers themselves, that the charges were unproven and Christ innocent — the Roman governor, the appointed judge, testifying on the record to the guiltlessness of the prisoner. The witnesses observe that this leaves the rulers without excuse: their own chosen judge, having heard all their charges and examined the accused, pronounced him innocent; their persistence in seeking his death was now plainly against justice, condemned by the very verdict they had sought. The expositors draw the lesson on the establishing of Christ's innocence: the repeated, formal acquittals of Pilate (and Herod) set it beyond doubt that Christ died not as a criminal but as the innocent — that his death was the murder of the guiltless, the offering of the spotless sacrifice. The witnesses note the tragedy that follows: Pilate, having declared Christ innocent, ought to have released him; instead (the next verses) he proposes to chastise and release — a compromise that betrays the justice he had just affirmed. The expositors observe the providence: the formal record of Christ's innocence, made by his Gentile judge, stands as a perpetual witness that the Lamb was without blemish. The witnesses draw the application: the innocence of Christ, attested by his judges, is the ground of our faith — that he who knew no sin was made sin for us; and the rulers' rejection of so clear a verdict is the warning of hardened unbelief. The verse thus records Pilate's renewed public acquittal — no fault found touching the charges — teaching the formal testimony of the judge to Christ's innocence, the rulers left without excuse, and the establishing of the guiltlessness of the spotless sacrifice.
15:
"No, nor yet Herod: for I sent you to him; and, lo, nothing worthy of death is done unto him." The witnesses note the double acquittal by both judges. The Reformed Gill notes Herod, "a Jew, better versed in their religious affairs," had also examined Christ "and yet found no fault in him." Barnes presses the weight: "they had had every opportunity of proving the charges, first before Pilate and then before Herod... and yet, after all, he was declared by both to be innocent; there could be no better evidence that he was innocent." The witnesses note Pilate citing Herod's verdict too — nothing worthy of death found in Christ.

The witnesses note the verdict of both judges: neither Pilate nor Herod found anything worthy of death.

So the witnesses make the verse the double acquittal of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the unanswerable testimony to Christ's innocence, attested by both his judges, leaving no possible doubt of his guiltlessness. The witnesses note Pilate's appeal to Herod's verdict: "No, nor yet Herod: for I sent you to him; and, lo, nothing worthy of death is done unto him" — Herod too, having examined Christ, found no crime deserving death; so the two judges agree, both acquitting the prisoner. The expositors press the weight Barnes draws out: Christ had been tried before two judges in succession, the accusers given every opportunity to prove their charges before each; and both Pilate and Herod, after full hearing, declared him innocent — "there could be no better evidence that he was innocent." The witnesses observe the force of Herod's testimony in particular: Herod, a Jew, versed in the religious matters that underlay the charges, and no friend to Jesus, yet found nothing worthy of death — the verdict of one acquainted with Jewish law and hostile to Christ, confirming his innocence. The expositors draw the lesson on the establishing of Christ's guiltlessness: the agreement of two judges, of differing nations and dispositions, both pronouncing him innocent, sets his innocence beyond all dispute — that he died not for any crime, but as the spotless Lamb. The witnesses note the irony and the injustice: with both judges acquitting, justice demanded release; yet the clamour of the rulers would prevail over the double verdict, condemning the One twice declared innocent. The expositors observe the providence: the double acquittal, recorded by the evangelist, stands as a witness for all ages that Christ was without fault — that his death was the offering of the innocent for the guilty. The witnesses draw the application: the innocence of Christ, doubly attested, is the foundation of his atoning sacrifice; the sinless One, found guiltless by his own judges, was condemned in our place. The verse thus records the double acquittal of Christ — neither Pilate nor Herod finding anything worthy of death — teaching the unanswerable testimony to his innocence, the agreement of his judges, and the establishing of the guiltlessness of the Lamb condemned in our stead.
16:
"I will therefore chastise him, and release him." The witnesses note Pilate's unjust compromise. The Reformed Gill explains the motive: Pilate proposed the scourging "not because he thought him deserving of it, but in complaisance to the Jews; since it would look as if their charges were not altogether groundless... and he hoped this might be thought sufficient, and so save Jesus from dying." Barnes presses the injustice: "it is not easy to see the reason why, if Pilate supposed Jesus to be innocent, he should propose to scourge him; it was as really unjust to do that as to crucify him." The witnesses note Pilate's compromise — to scourge the innocent and release him, hoping to satisfy the accusers.

The witnesses note Pilate's unjust compromise: to scourge the innocent Christ in hope of releasing him.

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's unjust compromise. The lesson the expositors draw is the injustice and weakness of Pilate, who, knowing Christ innocent, proposed to punish him to placate his accusers. The witnesses note the proposal: "I will therefore chastise him, and release him" — Pilate, having twice declared Christ innocent, yet, fearing the rulers, offers a compromise: to scourge him (a severe Roman punishment) and then release him, hoping this show of punishment would satisfy the accusers and save Christ's life. The expositors press the injustice Barnes marks: if Pilate believed Christ innocent — as he had just declared — to scourge him was as unjust as to crucify him; the punishment of the guiltless is wrong, whether by whipping or by death; the proposal itself a violation of the justice Pilate professed. The witnesses observe the weakness and the motive Gill names: Pilate proposed the scourging not because Christ deserved it, but "in complaisance to the Jews" — to make it appear their charges had some weight, and to offer them a partial satisfaction, hoping to escape the worse injustice of condemning Christ to death. The expositors draw the lesson on the fatal compromise: Pilate, instead of doing the right and releasing the innocent outright, sought a middle way — to do a lesser injustice to avoid the greater; but the way of compromise with evil leads only deeper into it, and Pilate's half-measure would not satisfy the rulers, but only embolden them to demand the cross. The witnesses note the warning in this: the judge who will not do full justice, but compromises with wrong to please men, finds no safe footing; his concession invites further demand, and his weakness draws him on to the very crime he sought to avoid. The expositors observe the contrast with Christ: the innocent Saviour, who had done no wrong, is proposed for scourging by the judge who acquitted him — the injustice of men heaping on the Lamb the punishment he did not deserve. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned against the compromise that, to please men, does a lesser wrong; and is to mark how Pilate's weakness, step by step, led to the condemnation of the innocent. The verse thus records Pilate's unjust compromise — to scourge and release the innocent — teaching the injustice of punishing the guiltless, the weakness of the judge who compromises with evil, and the fatal half-measure that would draw Pilate on to the cross.
17:
"(For of necessity he must release one unto them at the feast.)" The witnesses note the custom of the Passover release and a textual question. Clarke explains the custom: "he was under the necessity of releasing one at this feast; the custom, however it originated, had now been so completely established that Pilate was obliged to attend to it." Ellicott notes the textual difficulty: "the better MSS are singularly divided; most omit it altogether," suggesting "the narrative was originally written without it," and that it was added as an explanatory note (since the release of Barabbas required explanation) that "found its way into the text." The witnesses note the Passover custom of releasing a prisoner, which framed the choice between Christ and Barabbas.

The witnesses note the Passover custom of releasing a prisoner — the occasion of the fatal choice.

So the witnesses make the verse the custom of the Passover release. The lesson the expositors draw is the occasion this custom gave for the dreadful choice between Christ and Barabbas, and the honest noting of a textual question. The witnesses note the custom: "of necessity he must release one unto them at the feast" — a Passover practice (Clarke notes its origin obscure but its observance now fixed) by which the governor released a prisoner to the people each year; a gesture of clemency at the feast. The expositors observe how this custom framed the choice: Pilate, seeking a way to release Christ, hoped to use the custom — offering the people their choice, and expecting they would ask for Jesus; but the rulers turned it against him, demanding Barabbas instead (the verses to come). The witnesses note the textual question Ellicott raises with care: the verse is absent from many of the best manuscripts, and variously placed in others; Ellicott judges it likely an explanatory note (added because the release of Barabbas needed explaining) that entered the text — though the custom itself is firmly attested in Matthew and Mark (Matthew 27:15; Mark 15:6). The expositors draw the lesson, distinguishing the custom from the verse: whatever the textual status of Luke's parenthesis, the Passover custom of release is certain, recorded by the other evangelists; and it provided the occasion for the fatal choice the people would make. The witnesses press the providence in the custom: God permitted this practice to become the means by which the people would openly choose Barabbas over Christ — a murderer over the Prince of life — displaying the depth of their rejection and the perversity of their choice. The expositors observe the honesty of the witnesses in noting the manuscript evidence: the truth is not served by hiding textual difficulties, and the custom stands firm on the testimony of the other Gospels, whatever the place of this verse. The witnesses draw the application: the believer may hold with confidence the substance of the gospel history, attested across the Gospels, even where a particular verse is textually uncertain. The verse thus records the custom of the Passover release — the occasion of the choice between Christ and Barabbas — teaching the providential framing of the people's fatal choice, and the honest noting of a textual question that leaves the certain custom untouched.
18:
"And they cried out all at once, saying, Away with this man, and release unto us Barabbas:" The witnesses note the people's terrible cry. The grammatical-historical Robertson explains "all together" (a rare adverb) and "Away (with him)" — "take him away and keep him away" (as later of Paul, Acts 21:36; 22:22). Gill notes they would not bear to hear of Christ's release, "now they had got him in their hands." The witnesses note the whole multitude crying with one voice for Christ's removal and Barabbas's release.

The witnesses note the people's united cry: rejecting Christ and demanding the release of Barabbas.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's terrible choice. The lesson the expositors draw is the perversity and unanimity of the rejection of Christ, the people crying with one voice for a murderer over the Prince of life. The witnesses note the cry: "Away with this man, and release unto us Barabbas" — the whole multitude, with one voice (Robertson notes the rare word "all together"), demanding Christ's removal and the release of Barabbas; "away with him," they cry, take him off and be rid of him. The expositors press the perversity of the choice: offered the choice between Jesus and Barabbas, the people chose the murderer and rejected the Saviour — preferring a robber and insurrectionist (the next verse) to the holy, innocent Christ; the depth of their hardness exposed in so dreadful a preference. The witnesses observe the unanimity Gill marks: now that they had Christ in their power, they would not hear of his release, but cried out together, the rulers stirring up the people, the whole multitude joined in the rejection — a fearful unanimity in evil. The expositors draw the lesson on the rejection of Christ: this choice is the type of all rejection of the Saviour — men preferring the world, sin, and even a murderer to Christ; the heart, left to itself, choosing Barabbas over Jesus, darkness over light. The witnesses note the dreadful exchange the providence overruled: Barabbas, the guilty, is released; Christ, the innocent, is condemned — a picture of the gospel, the guilty set free and the innocent dying in his place; Barabbas the first, in a sense, to go free because Christ took his cross. The expositors observe the fulfilment of the people's own self-condemnation: in choosing Barabbas and rejecting Christ, the nation sealed its guilt, crying for the death of its Messiah. The witnesses draw the application: every soul faces, in its measure, the choice of Pilate's crowd — Christ or Barabbas, the Saviour or the world; and the rejection of Christ is the perversity of the fallen heart, which the believer is warned to abhor. The verse thus records the people's terrible cry — away with Christ, release Barabbas — teaching the perversity and unanimity of the rejection of Christ, the dreadful preference of a murderer over the Saviour, and the providential exchange of the guilty freed and the innocent condemned.
19:
"(Who for a certain sedition made in the city, and for murder, was cast into prison.)" The witnesses note the character of Barabbas. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the irony: Barabbas was imprisoned for "insurrection (the very charge made against Jesus, and untrue) and for murder; if Jesus had raised insurrection against Caesar, these accusers would have rallied to his standard." He adds bitingly that "the murderer was counted a hero" by some. Ellicott notes John calls Barabbas "a robber," Matthew "a notable prisoner." The witnesses note Barabbas a real insurrectionist and murderer — guilty of the very crimes falsely charged on Christ.

The witnesses note the irony: Barabbas truly guilty of sedition and murder — the crimes falsely laid on Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the character of Barabbas. The lesson the expositors draw is the bitter irony of the exchange — a real insurrectionist and murderer preferred to the innocent Christ, who was falsely charged with the very crimes Barabbas had truly committed. The witnesses note Barabbas's record: "for a certain sedition made in the city, and for murder, was cast into prison" — a genuine rebel and murderer, imprisoned for the real crimes of insurrection and bloodshed. The expositors press the irony Robertson marks: Barabbas was guilty of the very sedition against Caesar that was falsely charged on Christ; had Jesus truly raised such an insurrection, the accusers (who hated Roman rule) would have rallied to him, not condemned him — their charge against Christ was a lie, while Barabbas was the real seditionist. The witnesses observe the dreadful exchange this lays bare: the people chose the actual murderer and rebel over the innocent Prince of peace; the one truly guilty of the crimes alleged was set free, while the One falsely accused of them was condemned — the perversity of the choice exposed in the character of the man preferred. The expositors draw the lesson on the substitution pictured here: Barabbas, the guilty, goes free because Christ takes the cross that should have been his; the murderer released, the innocent dying — a vivid figure of the gospel, in which the guilty sinner is set at liberty because Christ bears his condemnation. The witnesses note Robertson's biting observation that the murderer was, to some, counted a hero — the people's sympathies perverted, preferring the violent rebel to the gentle Saviour; the moral blindness of a hardened crowd. The expositors observe the fulfilment of the deeper truth: Christ was "numbered with the transgressors" (Luke 22:37), condemned in the place of the guilty, that the guilty might go free — Barabbas the first and most literal instance of the exchange the cross would accomplish for all who believe. The witnesses draw the application: every believer is, in a sense, Barabbas — the guilty set free because the innocent Christ died in his stead; let him marvel at the exchange and abhor the choice that preferred the murderer to the Lord. The verse thus records the character of Barabbas — a real seditionist and murderer — teaching the bitter irony of the exchange, the perversity of preferring the guilty to the innocent, and the figure of the gospel in the guilty freed because Christ bore his cross.
20:
"Pilate therefore, willing to release Jesus, spake again to them." The witnesses note Pilate's renewed effort to free Christ. The Reformed Gill notes Pilate was "being more and more convinced of his innocence; and still seeing more clearly into the wickedness, malice, and envy of his accusers; and having received a message from his wife" (Matthew 27:19). The witnesses note Pilate, still desiring to release Christ, speaking again to the crowd to that end.

The witnesses note Pilate's continued desire to release Christ — speaking again on his behalf.

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's renewed effort to release Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of Pilate's conviction of Christ's innocence, struggling against his fear of the crowd. The witnesses note that Pilate, "willing to release Jesus, spake again to them" — still desiring to free the prisoner he had twice acquitted, he addressed the crowd once more, seeking to turn them from their demand. The expositors press the grounds of his persistence Gill names: Pilate was more and more convinced of Christ's innocence, saw ever more clearly the malice and envy of the accusers, and had received the warning of his wife's dream (Matthew 27:19) — all pressing him to release Christ. The witnesses observe the genuine struggle in Pilate: he was not, like the rulers, set on Christ's death, but truly wished to free him; his conscience and his judgment both told him Christ was innocent, and he made repeated efforts to release him. The expositors draw the lesson on the conflict in Pilate's soul: between the justice he knew and the fear he felt, between his conviction of Christ's innocence and his dread of the crowd and of Caesar; a conflict that exposes the weakness of a man who knows the right but lacks the courage to do it against opposition. The witnesses note the providence that, despite Pilate's wishes, Christ would yet be condemned — for the appointed sacrifice must be offered, and the very efforts of Pilate to release him serve to display both Christ's innocence and the inexcusable malice of those who would not be turned. The expositors observe the tragedy approaching: Pilate's repeated efforts, sincere as they were, would fail before the clamour of the crowd, for he wanted the courage to match his conviction; and the judge willing to release would, in the end, deliver Christ to death. The witnesses draw the application: good intentions and inward conviction are not enough without the courage to act on them; Pilate wished to free Christ, yet condemned him, for he feared men more than he loved justice. The verse thus records Pilate's renewed effort to release Christ — speaking again on his behalf — teaching the persistence of his conviction of Christ's innocence, the genuine struggle in his soul, and the weakness that, lacking courage, would yet fail to do the right he desired.
21:
"But they cried, saying, Crucify him, crucify him." The witnesses note the crowd's furious demand for crucifixion. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the imperfect "they kept on yelling," and the present imperative "go on with the crucifixion" (where Mark has the aorist, "do it now"). Gill notes "they were fiercer and more clamorous, the more they perceived Pilate was for saving him." The witnesses note the crowd, more enraged as Pilate sought to release Christ, crying out for his crucifixion.

The witnesses note the crowd's relentless cry: "Crucify him" — demanding the cruelest death for the innocent.

So the witnesses make the verse the crowd's furious demand for crucifixion. The lesson the expositors draw is the relentless fury of the rejection of Christ, the crowd demanding not merely his death but the cruelest and most shameful death. The witnesses note the cry: "Crucify him, crucify him" — repeated, insistent (Robertson marks the continued yelling); the crowd, far from being turned by Pilate's effort to release Christ, only grew fiercer, demanding crucifixion. The expositors press the significance of the demand: they cried not merely for Christ's death, but specifically for crucifixion — the most cruel, painful, and shameful of deaths, the Roman penalty for slaves and the worst criminals; their malice would have for him the utmost in suffering and disgrace. The witnesses observe Gill's note on the rising fury: the more Pilate sought to save Christ, the more clamorous the crowd became — their rage feeding on his reluctance, their demand hardening against his hesitation; opposition to their will only inflaming their hatred. The expositors draw the lesson on the depth of the rejection: the people, offered the release of Christ, demand instead his crucifixion — the rejection of the Saviour reaching its most violent expression, the cry for his blood drowning the voice of justice. The witnesses note the providence even in the cry: the crowd's demand for crucifixion, malicious as it was, fulfilled the appointed manner of Christ's death — the lifting up he had foretold (John 12:32-33), the death of the cross by which he would draw all men to himself, and the fulfilment of the Scriptures that spoke of pierced hands and feet (Psalm 22:16). The expositors observe the dreadful contrast: the crowd that days before had cried "Hosanna" now cries "Crucify"; the fickleness and perversity of the mob, turned by their rulers against the One they had hailed. The witnesses draw the application: the rejection of Christ, when it hardens, knows no bounds, demanding his utter destruction; and the believer is to mark the fury of the fallen heart against the Saviour, and the providence that overruled even the cry for the cross to accomplish redemption. The verse thus records the crowd's furious demand for crucifixion — "Crucify him" — teaching the relentless fury of the rejection of Christ, the demand for the cruelest death, and the providence overruling the cry to fulfil the appointed death of the cross.
22:
"And he said unto them the third time, Why, what evil hath he done? I have found no cause of death in him: I will therefore chastise him, and let him go." The witnesses note Pilate's third declaration of innocence. Clarke presses the legal weight: "here our blessed Lord was in the most formal manner justified by his judge... as this decision was publicly known and perhaps registered, it is evident that Christ died as an innocent person, and not as a malefactor; on the fullest conviction of his innocence, his judge pronounced him guiltless." The witnesses note Pilate, a third time, declaring Christ innocent and proposing again to chastise and release him.

The witnesses note Pilate's third acquittal: "What evil hath he done? I find no cause of death."

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's third declaration of Christ's innocence. The lesson the expositors draw is the formal, thrice-repeated justification of Christ by his judge, establishing beyond all doubt that he died as the innocent. The witnesses note Pilate's third appeal: "Why, what evil hath he done? I have found no cause of death in him" — challenging the crowd to name any crime, and declaring again that he found nothing deserving death; the third time he pronounces Christ innocent. The expositors press the legal weight Clarke draws out: Christ was, in the most formal manner, justified by his judge — publicly, repeatedly, perhaps on the official record; so that it stands established for all time that Christ died as an innocent person, not as a malefactor, on the fullest conviction of his judge. The witnesses observe the threefold acquittal: three times (verses 4, 14-15, and here) Pilate declared Christ guiltless — a solemn, formal, repeated testimony that leaves no room for doubt; the Lamb thrice pronounced without blemish by the very judge appointed to try him. The expositors draw the lesson on the establishing of Christ's innocence: the repeated, public acquittals are the witness of God's providence that Christ was sinless, that his death was no punishment for his own guilt but the offering of the innocent for the guilty — "who did no sin" (1 Peter 2:22), condemned not by justice but by malice. The witnesses note the persistence of Pilate's weakness: a third time he proposes the unjust compromise — "I will chastise him, and let him go" — still seeking to placate the crowd by scourging the innocent, still lacking the courage to release him outright; his conviction firm, his will weak. The expositors observe the contrast between Pilate's words and his coming deed: he declares Christ innocent, yet will deliver him to death; the judge who thrice acquits will yet condemn — the tragedy of conviction without courage. The witnesses draw the application: Christ's innocence, thrice attested by his judge, is the firm ground of the gospel; and Pilate's repeated failure to act on his conviction is the warning of a conscience overborne by fear. The verse thus records Pilate's third declaration of innocence — "what evil hath he done?" — teaching the formal, thrice-repeated justification of Christ, the establishing of his guiltlessness on the record, and the persistence of Pilate's weakness in compromising with the crowd.
23:
"And they were instant with loud voices, requiring that he might be crucified. And the voices of them and of the chief priests prevailed." The witnesses note the clamour overwhelming Pilate. The grammatical-historical Robertson vividly renders it: "they pressed upon him... the imperfect of a verb for the rush and swirl of a tempest; poor Pilate was overwhelmed by this tornado; the tempest Pilate had invited." Ellicott notes "instant" means "urgent" (as "instant in prayer," Romans 12:12). The witnesses note the crowd's loud, insistent demand prevailing over Pilate's weak resistance.

The witnesses note the clamour prevailing: the crowd's loud insistence overwhelming Pilate's resistance.

So the witnesses make the verse the triumph of the clamour over Pilate. The lesson the expositors draw is the overwhelming of weak justice by determined clamour, and the fatal yielding of the judge who had invited the conflict. The witnesses note the scene: "they were instant with loud voices" — urgent, pressing, insistent (Ellicott marks "instant" as "urgent"), demanding crucifixion; and Robertson vividly pictures it as a tempest, a tornado, sweeping over the irresolute Pilate. The expositors press the outcome: "the voices of them and of the chief priests prevailed" — the clamour won; the loud, relentless demand of the crowd and the rulers overbore Pilate's weak resistance, and he yielded. The witnesses observe Robertson's biting note that "the tempest Pilate had invited": by his compromises and his fear, by offering the choice and the scourging instead of doing justice, Pilate had invited the very storm that now overwhelmed him; his own weakness gave the clamour its power. The expositors draw the lesson on the defeat of justice by clamour: where the judge lacks the courage of his conviction, the loudest and most determined voices prevail; Pilate knew the right, declared it thrice, yet yielded to the shouting — justice overborne not by argument but by noise and persistence. The witnesses note the warning in this: the will of the mob, the pressure of clamour, the fear of the crowd, can overwhelm a weak conscience; and the judge who will not stand firm for the right is swept away by the tempest he failed to resist at the first. The expositors observe the tragedy: the innocent Christ, thrice acquitted, is condemned not by any proof of guilt but by the prevailing of loud voices — the triumph of clamour over truth, of the mob over the judge. The witnesses note the providence even here: the prevailing of the clamour accomplished the appointed death of Christ, the very malice and weakness of men serving the purpose of God in the offering of the Lamb. The expositors draw the application: the believer is warned against the cowardice that yields to clamour, and is to mark how the Saviour was condemned by the prevailing of voices, not by justice. The verse thus records the triumph of the clamour over Pilate — the loud voices prevailing — teaching the overwhelming of weak justice by determined demand, the fatal yielding of the judge who invited the storm, and the providence overruling the clamour to accomplish redemption.
24:
"And Pilate gave sentence that it should be as they required." The witnesses note Pilate's fatal sentence. Cyril of Alexandria draws the lesson on the folly of the synagogue: "a disgraceful malady, my brethren, is want of understanding and folly of heart, accompanied by the inventions of base thoughts, which lead men on to everything wicked, and often make us sin against the glory of God." Gill notes Pilate pronounced the sentence "according to their request." The witnesses note Pilate, yielding to the clamour, formally sentencing Christ to be crucified as the crowd demanded.

The witnesses note Pilate's surrender: pronouncing sentence according to the crowd's demand.

So the witnesses make the verse Pilate's fatal sentence. The lesson the expositors draw is the surrender of justice to the will of the mob, Pilate condemning the innocent against his own conviction. The witnesses note the dreadful sentence: "Pilate gave sentence that it should be as they required" — yielding at last to the clamour, the judge pronounced the formal sentence of crucifixion, not according to justice, but according to the demand of the crowd; the innocent condemned to satisfy the rage of his accusers. The expositors press the injustice and the surrender: Pilate, who had thrice declared Christ innocent, now sentences him to death — not because any guilt was proved, but because the people required it; the judge abdicating his office, surrendering his judgment to the mob, giving the verdict the crowd dictated. The witnesses observe Robertson's earlier note that this was "mob law by the judge who surrenders his own power and justice to the clamour of the crowd" — the very negation of justice, the sentence determined not by the evidence but by the loudest voices. The expositors draw the lesson Cyril presses on the folly of the heart: the synagogue, in its want of understanding and folly, led by base thoughts, was carried on to the greatest of crimes — sinning against the glory of God in condemning his Son; a disgraceful malady of the heart that ends in the murder of the Saviour. The witnesses note the warning in Pilate's surrender: the judge who yields to clamour against his conscience commits the gravest injustice; Pilate's fear of men, his lack of courage, his fatal compromises, all issue at last in the sentence of the innocent — the path of cowardice ending in the condemnation of Christ. The expositors observe the providence overruling the injustice: the unjust sentence of Pilate accomplished the just purpose of God, the offering of the Lamb for the sin of the world; the wickedness of men serving the appointed redemption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned against the cowardice and folly that, yielding to pressure, sin against God; and is to behold, in the unjust sentence of Christ, the price of his salvation. The verse thus records Pilate's fatal sentence — condemning Christ as the crowd required — teaching the surrender of justice to the mob, the folly of heart that condemned the Son of God, and the providence overruling the injustice to accomplish redemption.
25:
"And he released unto them him that for sedition and murder was cast into prison, whom they had desired; but he delivered Jesus to their will." The witnesses note the dreadful exchange completed. Gill notes Barabbas "is not named, as being a detestable person, and unworthy to be named." The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the injustice: "to their will — this is mob law by the judge who surrenders his own power and justice to the clamour of the crowd." The witnesses note the final exchange: Barabbas the guilty released, Christ the innocent delivered to the will of his enemies.

The witnesses note the exchange completed: the murderer freed, Christ delivered to the will of the crowd.

So the witnesses make the verse the completion of the dreadful exchange. The lesson the expositors draw is the consummation of the injustice — the guilty freed and the innocent delivered up — and the figure of the gospel it presents. The witnesses note the exchange: "he released unto them" Barabbas, "for sedition and murder cast into prison" (Gill notes the evangelist does not name him, as one too detestable to name), while "he delivered Jesus to their will" — the murderer set free, the innocent Christ handed over to the will of his enemies. The expositors press the injustice Robertson marks: "he delivered Jesus to their will" — not to justice, but to the will of the crowd; mob law triumphing, the judge surrendering the innocent to the malice of his accusers, to do with him as they would. The witnesses observe the dreadful contrast completed: Barabbas, guilty of the very crimes falsely charged on Christ, walks free; Christ, thrice declared innocent, is delivered to death — the guilty acquitted, the guiltless condemned; the perfect reversal of justice. The expositors draw the lesson on the figure of the gospel: in this exchange is pictured the heart of redemption — the guilty sinner (Barabbas, and every believer) set free because the innocent Christ is delivered up in his place; "he was delivered for our offences" (Romans 4:25), that we, the guilty, might go free. The witnesses note the literal first instance: Barabbas, condemned to die, is released because Christ takes the cross that would have been his — the most vivid and literal picture of substitution, one man set at liberty because another dies in his stead. The expositors observe the solemn phrase "delivered Jesus to their will": Christ given over to the will of his enemies, to be mocked, scourged, and crucified — the Lamb delivered up, yet (as the Scriptures declare) delivered also "by the determinate counsel of God" (Acts 2:23), the will of men and the purpose of God meeting in his death. The witnesses draw the application: every believer is Barabbas, the guilty freed because Christ was delivered up; let him marvel at the exchange, and abhor the will that demanded it. The verse thus records the completion of the exchange — Barabbas released, Christ delivered to their will — teaching the consummation of the injustice, the figure of the gospel in the guilty freed and the innocent condemned, and the Lamb delivered up for our redemption.
26:
"And as they led him away, they laid hold upon one Simon, a Cyrenian, coming out of the country, and on him they laid the cross, that he might bear it after Jesus." The witnesses note Simon compelled to bear the cross and its harmony with John. Augustine harmonizes: John says Christ "bore His own cross... when He went forth," but "as they journeyed Simon was forced into the service on the road" — Christ bearing it first, then Simon compelled to carry it the rest of the way. Theophylact notes the cross was counted "an abomination," so Simon was made to bear "to his dishonour" what others refused; and sees fulfilled Isaiah's "the government shall be upon his shoulder" (Isaiah 9:6) — "the government of Christ is His cross." The witnesses note Simon of Cyrene compelled to carry the cross behind Jesus.

The witnesses note Simon compelled to bear the cross behind Christ — following the Lord under his burden.

So the witnesses make the verse Simon bearing the cross. The lesson the expositors draw is the harmony of the Gospels, the dishonour and the honour of the cross, and the figure of cross-bearing discipleship. The witnesses note that, as Christ was led away, the soldiers "laid hold upon one Simon, a Cyrenian," and compelled him to bear the cross after Jesus. The expositors observe Augustine's harmony with John (who says Christ bore his own cross): Christ carried the cross first, going forth from the judgment-hall; but on the road, his strength failing under the burden and the scourging, the soldiers compelled Simon to bear it the rest of the way — both accounts true, the one of the start, the other of the journey. The witnesses note Theophylact's twofold observation: the cross was counted an abomination, a thing of shame, so that none would willingly bear it, and Simon was forced to carry it "to his dishonour" — yet in this very bearing is fulfilled Isaiah's word, "the government shall be upon his shoulder" (Isaiah 9:6), for "the government of Christ is His cross," the throne of his saving reign.
