The expositors draw the lesson on the cross as Christ's glory: what the world counted shame is Christ's dignity, the cross his royal burden, the instrument of his triumph; the dishonour of the cross become the badge of his sovereignty. The witnesses press the figure of discipleship in Simon: compelled to "bear it after Jesus," Simon is the type of the disciple who takes up the cross and follows Christ (Luke 9:23) — bearing the burden behind his Lord, sharing in his reproach; and the expositors note the providence and grace in Simon's compulsion, for tradition holds he and his sons became believers (Mark 15:21, "the father of Alexander and Rufus"), the forced burden turning to his blessing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is called, like Simon, to bear the cross after Jesus — to follow his Lord under the burden of reproach and suffering, counting the shame an honour, the cross the way of the kingdom. The verse thus records Simon bearing the cross — compelled to follow Christ under its weight — teaching the harmony of the Gospels, the dishonour become dignity in the cross of Christ, and the figure of the disciple who takes up the cross and follows his Lord.
27:
"And there followed him a great company of people, and of women, which also bewailed and lamented him." The witnesses note the mourning women, a detail peculiar to Luke. Ellicott marks it "a characteristic incident peculiar to St. Luke, obviously derived from the devout women to whom we have traced so many facts which he alone records"; the "daughters of Jerusalem... perhaps one of the sisterhoods formed in that city for mitigating" the sufferings of the condemned. Clarke notes "bewailed and lamented" means they "beat their breasts." Gill notes the crowd was mixed — some pitying, some mocking. The witnesses note the great company following Christ, the women bewailing him.

The witnesses note the women of Jerusalem following Christ to the cross, weeping and lamenting him.

So the witnesses make the verse the mourning women. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender compassion of the women who followed Christ, and the distinctive witness of Luke to their lament. The witnesses note the scene: "a great company of people, and of women" followed Christ as he was led to the cross — a mixed multitude (Gill notes some pitying, some mocking), and among them women who "bewailed and lamented him," beating their breasts (Clarke) in grief. The expositors observe the detail peculiar to Luke that Ellicott marks: the mourning of the women is recorded only by Luke, the evangelist of compassion, who (Ellicott suggests) drew so many of his details from the devout women among Christ's followers; the "daughters of Jerusalem," perhaps a sisterhood formed to show kindness to the condemned, lamenting the suffering Christ. The witnesses press the tenderness of the women's grief: amid the cruelty of the soldiers and the malice of the rulers, the women alone show open compassion, weeping for the suffering Saviour — a touch of humanity and pity in the dark scene of the crucifixion-march. The expositors draw the lesson, with the qualification Christ himself would add (the next verse): the women's compassion was real and tender, yet partly misdirected — they wept for Christ's sufferings as for a pitiable man, when the deeper cause of weeping was their own and their nation's coming judgment; Christ would turn their tears from himself to themselves. The witnesses note the honour Luke gives to the faithful women: throughout his Gospel, the women who followed and ministered to Christ are remembered, and here their lament is recorded when the men (save John) had fled — the devotion of the women conspicuous at the cross. The expositors observe the contrast with the mockers: while many reviled, the women wept; while the rulers triumphed, the daughters of Jerusalem lamented — the compassion of the few amid the cruelty of the many. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to follow Christ with compassion and devotion, as the women did; yet to weep, as Christ would teach, not merely over his sufferings but over the sin that caused them and the judgment it deserves. The verse thus records the mourning women — following Christ and lamenting him — teaching the tender compassion of the faithful women, the distinctive witness of Luke to their grief, and the devotion that followed the Saviour to the cross.
28:
"But Jesus turning unto them said, Daughters of Jerusalem, weep not for me, but weep for yourselves, and for your children." The witnesses note Christ turning the women's tears to the coming judgment. Barnes explains: "weep for yourselves... refers to the calamities that were about to come upon them in the desolation of their city by the Romans." Clarke draws the deeper lesson: "no human spirit can possibly take any part in the passion of the Messiah; His sufferings were such as only God manifested in the flesh could bear, and, as they were all of an expiatory nature, no man can taste of or share in them." The witnesses note Christ, even on the way to the cross, forgetting his own sufferings to warn the women of the destruction of Jerusalem.

The witnesses note Christ's word to the women: weep not for me, but for yourselves and your children.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's turning of the women's tears to the coming judgment. The lesson the expositors draw is the selfless compassion of Christ, who, amid his own sufferings, warned the women of the greater calamity to come, and the truth that his sufferings are not merely to be pitied but to be received as expiatory. The witnesses note that Christ, "turning unto them," addressed the women: "Daughters of Jerusalem, weep not for me, but weep for yourselves, and for your children" — even on the way to the cross, bearing his own agony, he forgets himself to think of them, turning their tears from his sufferings to their own coming judgment. The expositors press the meaning Barnes draws: the calamity Christ foretold was the destruction of Jerusalem by the Romans (AD 70), the dreadful desolation in which the women and their children would suffer unspeakably; let them weep, not for him whose sufferings would issue in glory, but for themselves and their children, on whom the judgment for rejecting their Messiah would fall. The witnesses observe the selfless compassion of Christ: in the depth of his own suffering, his thought is for others — for the women, for the city, for the children who would bear the coming wrath; the Saviour weeping for Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) even as he goes to die for it. The expositors draw the deeper lesson Clarke presses: Christ's sufferings are not merely a subject for human pity, as if we could share or relieve them; they were of an expiatory nature, borne by God manifest in the flesh, such as no mere man could bear or take part in; therefore the right response is not to weep over them as over a pitiable man's woes, but to receive them as the atoning work of the Saviour, and to weep over the sin that made them necessary. The witnesses note the redirection of grief: Christ turns the women from a sentimental sorrow over his sufferings to a salutary sorrow over sin and its judgment — the true mourning being repentance for the sin that crucified him and brought judgment on the nation. The expositors observe the warning to all: the rejection of Christ brings judgment, and the tears that befit the cross are tears of repentance, not of mere pity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to weep, not with a barren sentiment over Christ's pains, but with godly sorrow over the sin that caused them, and with reverent faith to receive his expiatory work. The verse thus records Christ's word to the women — weep for yourselves — teaching his selfless compassion amid his sufferings, the warning of Jerusalem's coming judgment, and the truth that his sufferings are to be received as expiatory, not merely pitied.
29:
"For, behold, the days are coming, in the which they shall say, Blessed are the barren, and the wombs that never bare, and the paps which never gave suck." The witnesses note the dreadful reversal foretold for Jerusalem. Ellicott marks the strangeness of the beatitude: "we must enter into all the passionate desire of Israelite women for offspring (as in Rachel, Genesis 30:1, and Hannah, 1 Samuel 1:10-11) to estimate the strangeness of such a beatitude" — childlessness, once the deepest grief, now called blessed. Robertson notes "a beatitude to the barren, the opposite of the hopes of Jewish mothers." Calvin sees a calamity "not usual, but fearful and unheard of." The witnesses note Christ foretelling so terrible a destruction that the childless would be counted happy.

The witnesses note the foretold horror: a day so dreadful that barrenness, once mourned, would be called blessed.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's foretelling of Jerusalem's calamity. The lesson the expositors draw is the unimaginable severity of the coming judgment, in which the deepest sorrows of nature would be reckoned mercies. The witnesses note the dreadful reversal: "blessed are the barren" — a beatitude that, Ellicott and Robertson press, overturns the deepest desire of Israelite women, for whom childlessness was the bitterest grief and motherhood the highest blessing (Genesis 30:1; 1 Samuel 1:10-11). The expositors observe the horror this implies: in the coming destruction of Jerusalem by the Romans (AD 70), the calamity would be so extreme that those without children would be happier than mothers — for they would not see their little ones perish in the famine, the sword, and the horrors of the siege; barrenness, once mourned, becoming a mercy. The witnesses press the severity Calvin marks: a calamity not usual but fearful and unheard of — the unspeakable miseries of the siege, when (as Josephus records) mothers suffered the death of their children and worse; a judgment beyond ordinary woe. The expositors draw the lesson on the wages of rejecting Christ: this was the judgment that would fall on the nation for the rejection of its Messiah; the city that crucified the Lord would itself be destroyed, its sufferings the recompense of its sin. The witnesses note Christ's compassion even in the warning: he turns from his own sufferings (v.28) to weep over the coming woes of the daughters of Jerusalem, foretelling their grief that they might be warned — the Saviour mourning the doom of those who slew him. The expositors observe the solemnity: the beatitude of the barren is the measure of the horror, a judgment so dreadful that the absence of what was most desired became the only mercy. The witnesses draw the application: the rejection of Christ brings judgment of terrible severity; and the believer is to weep, not over Christ's sufferings only, but over the sin and its consequences, fleeing the wrath to come. The verse thus records Christ's foretelling of Jerusalem's calamity — the barren called blessed — teaching the unimaginable severity of the judgment for rejecting the Messiah, and the compassion of Christ who warned even those who crucified him.
30:
"Then shall they begin to say to the mountains, Fall on us; and to the hills, Cover us." The witnesses note the despairing cry foretold. The Reformed Gill notes the tribulation would be so great that "they will seek to go into the holes of the rocks and caves of the earth," as Josephus records of the siege, choosing rather to be buried than to endure. Robertson marks it a quotation from Hosea 10:8. Lightfoot connects it to the like cry in Revelation 6:16. The witnesses note the people, in their extremity, longing for the mountains to fall and bury them.

The witnesses note the cry of despair: a people begging the mountains to fall and end their misery.

So the witnesses make the verse the despairing cry of the doomed. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of the misery foretold, in which death would be sought as a relief, and the prophetic pattern of judgment. The witnesses note the cry: "to the mountains, Fall on us; and to the hills, Cover us" — the people, in the horror of the siege, would long for the mountains to fall and crush them, preferring sudden death and burial under the rocks to the prolonged agony of the destruction. The expositors observe Gill's note from history: Josephus records that many in the siege did indeed seek the caves and holes of the earth, hiding and perishing; the cry for the mountains to cover them the expression of a misery so great that annihilation seemed a mercy. The witnesses press the prophetic background Robertson marks: the words echo Hosea 10:8, where the like cry is foretold as the language of judgment; Christ applies the prophet's words to the coming doom of Jerusalem. The expositors note Lightfoot's connection to Revelation 6:16, where the same cry returns as the language of the wicked at the great day of wrath — "fall on us, and hide us from the face of him that sitteth on the throne"; so the cry of doomed Jerusalem prefigures the cry of all the impenitent under final judgment. The witnesses draw the lesson on the terror of judgment: when the wrath of God falls, men seek death and cannot find it, longing for the mountains to hide them from a judgment worse than death; the misery of the lost beyond the relief even of the grave. The expositors observe the warning to all: the cry of Jerusalem is the cry of every soul that meets the wrath of God unprepared; better to flee to Christ now than to call on the mountains then. The witnesses note the contrast with the believer's hope: while the impenitent cry for the mountains to cover them, the believer looks to Christ to shelter him from the wrath to come. The verse thus records the despairing cry foretold — the mountains begged to fall — teaching the depth of the misery in judgment, the prophetic pattern from Hosea to Revelation, and the warning to flee the wrath while mercy may be found.
31:
"For if they do these things in a green tree, what shall be done in the dry?" The witnesses note the proverb of the green and dry tree. The Reformed Gill reads the green tree as Christ himself: "the metaphor seems designed to express the righteousness and innocence of Christ" — green as full of grace and life, "not proper to be cut down, nor fit fuel for the fire," yet so treated; "if they do these things" to the innocent Christ, "what shall be done" to the guilty nation, the dry tree fit for burning. Lightfoot discusses the saying's force. The witnesses note Christ contrasting his own innocent suffering with the deserved judgment to come on the guilty.

The witnesses note the proverb: if the innocent green tree is so treated, what of the guilty, dry one?

So the witnesses make the verse the proverb of the green and dry tree. The lesson the expositors draw is the argument from the lesser to the greater: if the innocent Christ suffered so, far worse awaits the guilty nation. The witnesses note the figure Gill draws out: the "green tree" is Christ — full of life and grace, flourishing in his ministry, innocent and fruitful, in no way deserving to be cut down or burned; and the "dry tree" is the guilty, the impenitent nation, fit fuel for the fire of judgment. The expositors press the force of the argument: "if they do these things in a green tree" — if the Jews and Romans so treat the innocent, holy Christ, crucifying the guiltless Lamb — "what shall be done in the dry?" — what judgment must fall on the guilty nation, ripe for destruction, when even the innocent was not spared. The witnesses observe the lesson from lesser to greater: if God spared not his own Son, who bore the sin of others, when he stood in the sinner's place, how much less will he spare the impenitent who bear their own sin; if such suffering came on the green tree, the dry tree cannot escape the fire. The expositors draw out the application to Jerusalem: the city that crucified its Messiah, the dry tree without the sap of grace, would be given to the burning — the destruction of AD 70 the judgment on the guilty, far exceeding even the sufferings of the innocent Christ. The witnesses note the broader principle: if judgment begins at the house of God, and the righteous are chastened, what shall be the end of the ungodly (1 Peter 4:17-18); the sufferings of the innocent a warning of the far greater judgment awaiting the guilty. The expositors observe the solemnity of the warning: Christ, going to his own death, warns the daughters of Jerusalem that his sufferings, terrible as they were, are but the green tree compared to the dry — the coming judgment on the nation would be worse. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to mark the certainty and severity of judgment on the impenitent, argued from the sufferings of the innocent Christ; and to flee to him who, the green tree, bore the fire for the dry. The verse thus records the proverb of the green and dry tree — teaching the argument from lesser to greater, that if the innocent Christ so suffered, far worse awaits the guilty, and the certainty of judgment on the impenitent nation.
32:
"And there were also two other, malefactors, led with him to be put to death." The witnesses note the two criminals crucified with Christ. Cyril of Alexandria connects it to the fulfilment of Isaiah's word that he was numbered with the transgressors. Gill and the witnesses note the two malefactors led out with Christ to die — the innocent placed among the guilty. The witnesses note Christ crucified between two criminals, fulfilling prophecy.

The witnesses note the two malefactors led with Christ — the innocent numbered with the guilty.

So the witnesses make the verse the two malefactors led with Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the fulfilment of prophecy in Christ's being numbered with transgressors, and the depth of his humiliation in dying among criminals. The witnesses note that "two other, malefactors" were "led with him to be put to death" — two condemned criminals brought out to be crucified alongside Christ; the innocent Lamb placed between two guilty men, as though one of them. The expositors press the fulfilment of Isaiah 53:12, which Christ himself had cited (Luke 22:37): "he was numbered with the transgressors" — crucified between two thieves, reckoned among the guilty, sharing the death of malefactors; the prophecy exactly accomplished in the manner of his execution. The witnesses observe the depth of the humiliation: not only was Christ crucified, the death of slaves and the worst criminals, but he was crucified in the company of malefactors, set among them as one of their number — the spotless One treated as the chief of sinners, the holy Lamb dying between two robbers. The expositors draw the lesson on the substitution: Christ was numbered with transgressors because he bore the place of transgressors, dying in the sinner's stead; his being crucified among the guilty a visible sign that he was made sin for us, taking the place of the condemned. The witnesses note the providence in the arrangement: the very manner of his death, devised by his enemies for his greater shame, fulfilled the Scriptures and displayed the truth of his atoning work — and, as the verses to come show, gave occasion for the salvation of the penitent thief, that even on the cross Christ might save a sinner. The expositors observe the contrast that would soon appear: the two malefactors, alike in their crime and their condemnation, would differ in their end — one railing and perishing, the other believing and saved; the cross between which Christ hung the dividing line of faith and unbelief. The witnesses draw the application: the believer beholds in Christ crucified among the guilty the Saviour who took the sinner's place, numbered with transgressors that transgressors might be numbered with the saints. The verse thus records the two malefactors led with Christ — the innocent among the guilty — teaching the fulfilment of prophecy in his being numbered with transgressors, the depth of his humiliation, and the substitution by which he took the place of the condemned.
33:
"And when they were come to the place, which is called Calvary, there they crucified him, and the malefactors, one on the right hand, and the other on the left." The witnesses note the place and meaning of the crucifixion. Athanasius draws the redemptive design: "when mankind became corrupted, then Christ manifested His own body, that where corruption had been seen, there might spring up incorruption; wherefore He is crucified in the place of Calvary, which the Jewish doctors say was the burial-place of Adam." Bede gives the name: "the places where the heads of condemned criminals were cut off received the name of Calvary, that is, the place of skulls"; and draws the lesson: "the innocent is crucified among the guilty, that where sin abounded, grace might much more abound." The witnesses note Christ crucified at Calvary between the two malefactors.

The witnesses note the crucifixion at Calvary — the innocent crucified among the guilty for the salvation of all.

So the witnesses make the verse the crucifixion of Christ at Calvary. The lesson the expositors draw is the redemptive meaning of the place and the manner — the innocent crucified among the guilty, that grace might abound where sin abounded. The witnesses note the bare, dreadful fact: "there they crucified him" — the Lord of glory nailed to the cross at the place called Calvary, between the two malefactors, one on each hand; the central act of the world's redemption stated in the fewest words. The expositors draw out the meaning of the place. Bede explains the name: Calvary, the "place of skulls," where condemned criminals were executed — a place of death and shame, fit setting for the death of the Lamb. Athanasius and the tradition add that Calvary was held to be the burial-place of Adam — so that, fittingly, where the first man's corruption lay, there the second Adam manifested his body, that incorruption might spring up where corruption had been; the death of Christ undoing, at the very spot, the death brought in by Adam. The witnesses press Bede's lesson on the company: "the innocent is crucified among the guilty, that where sin abounded, grace might much more abound" (Romans 5:20) — Christ placed between the two thieves, the sinless among sinners, that his grace might overflow to the guilty. The expositors observe the design in the position: Christ crucified in the midst, between the two, the central figure — and (as the verses show) dividing the two, the one on his right believing and saved, the one on his left railing and lost; the cross the throne of his saving and judging grace. The witnesses note the wonder of the redemptive exchange: the holy One crucified in the place of skulls, among malefactors, over Adam's grave — that death might be swallowed up in victory, and life spring from the place of death. The expositors draw the application: the believer beholds at Calvary the heart of redemption — the innocent dying among the guilty, grace abounding over sin, life conquering death at the very seat of death. The verse thus records the crucifixion at Calvary — the innocent among the guilty — teaching the redemptive meaning of the place of skulls, the abounding of grace over sin, and the cross as the throne of Christ's saving grace.
34:
"Then said Jesus, Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do. And they parted his raiment, and cast lots." The witnesses note Christ's prayer for his enemies, and debate for whom it was offered. Chrysostom sees Christ practising his own teaching: "because the Lord had said, Pray for them that persecute you, this He did when He ascended the cross... not that He was unable Himself to pardon them, but that He might teach us to pray for our persecutors, not only in word, but in deed." Bede limits the prayer's scope: "He prayed not for those who chose rather to crucify than confess Him whom they knew to be the Son of God, but for such as were ignorant... having a zeal for God, but not according to knowledge" — and the prayer was answered "in those who believed after His passion." The witnesses note the division of his garments fulfilling Psalm 22:18.

The witnesses note Christ's prayer of forgiveness for his ignorant persecutors, answered in those who later believed.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's prayer for his enemies. The lesson the expositors draw is the boundless love of Christ, praying for his murderers, and the careful scope and answer of that prayer. The witnesses note the first word from the cross: "Father, forgive them; for they know not what they do" — Christ, in the agony of crucifixion, prays not for himself but for his enemies, asking the Father to pardon those who nailed him to the tree. The expositors press the love this displays, which Chrysostom marks: Christ here practises what he taught ("pray for them that persecute you," Matthew 5:44) — not in word only but in deed, interceding for his persecutors in the very act of their cruelty; the supreme example of love to enemies. The witnesses observe the careful scope Bede draws out: the prayer was for those who acted in ignorance — the soldiers who knew not what they did, and the people who, having a zeal for God but not according to knowledge, did not recognize their Messiah; not for those who knowingly crucified the One they knew to be the Son of God, sinning against the light. The expositors weigh the answer of the prayer: Bede notes it was not offered in vain, but answered in the thousands who, convicted at Pentecost and after, believed and were forgiven (Acts 2:37-41) — the ignorant persecutors brought to repentance, the fruit of Christ's intercession. The witnesses note the ground of the plea, "for they know not what they do": their ignorance, though not innocence, was a ground for mercy; they knew not the full enormity of crucifying the Lord of glory, and Christ pleads their ignorance that they might be brought to repentance and pardon. The expositors draw the lesson on the intercession of Christ: he who prayed for his murderers ever lives to make intercession for transgressors (Isaiah 53:12; Hebrews 7:25); his prayer on the cross the pattern and pledge of his saving advocacy. The witnesses note the fulfilment of prophecy in the parting of his garments and the casting of lots (Psalm 22:18) — the soldiers, all unknowing, accomplishing the Scripture even as Christ prayed for their forgiveness. The verse thus records Christ's prayer for his enemies — forgive them, for they know not — teaching the boundless love of Christ to his persecutors, the scope of the prayer for the ignorant, and its answer in those who afterward believed.
35:
"And the people stood beholding. And the rulers also with them derided him, saying, He saved others; let him save himself, if he be Christ, the chosen of God." The witnesses note the people watching and the rulers' scornful taunt. Ellicott notes the verb "derided" "implies the curled lip and distended nostril of scorn," and that "He saved others" was, "like the words of Caiaphas (John 11:50), an unconscious" testimony to the truth. Gill notes the rulers, "whose characters should have restrained them," joined the insult. The witnesses note the rulers mocking Christ's saving power, challenging him to save himself.

The witnesses note the rulers' scornful taunt — mocking the Saviour to save himself, unwittingly confessing he saved others.

So the witnesses make the verse the rulers' derision of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the malice of the rulers, whose very taunt was an unconscious confession of the truth, and the design of Christ's not saving himself. The witnesses note the scene: "the people stood beholding" — the crowd watching the spectacle — while "the rulers also with them derided him," the chief priests, scribes, and elders, who should have been restrained by their office, joining in the mockery with scorn (Ellicott notes the verb implies the curled lip of contempt). The expositors press the taunt: "He saved others; let him save himself, if he be Christ, the chosen of God" — a jeer at his saving power, challenging him, if he were truly the Messiah, to deliver himself from the cross. The witnesses observe the unconscious testimony Ellicott marks: "He saved others" — the rulers, in their very mockery, confessed the truth, that Christ had indeed saved others, healed the sick, raised the dead; like Caiaphas's unwitting prophecy (John 11:50), their scorn proclaimed the fact they meant to deny. The expositors draw the lesson on the design of Christ's not saving himself: the taunt assumed that if he could not save himself, he was no Saviour; but the very opposite was true — he did not save himself precisely because he was saving others; had he come down from the cross, he could not have redeemed the world. The witnesses press the deep irony: the rulers thought his staying on the cross proved him no Christ, when in truth it was by staying on the cross that he accomplished the salvation he came to bring; he saved others, therefore he could not save himself, for the saving of others required his death. The expositors observe the malice and blindness of the rulers: they mocked the Saviour in his agony, blind to the salvation he was working before their eyes, their scorn the measure of their hardness. The witnesses draw the application: the cross, which the rulers derided as the proof of failure, was the place of victory; and the believer glories in the Christ who, to save others, would not save himself. The verse thus records the rulers' derision — let him save himself — teaching the malice of the rulers, the unconscious confession in their taunt, and the saving design by which Christ, to redeem others, refused to deliver himself.
36:
"And the soldiers also mocked him, coming to him, and offering him vinegar." The witnesses note the soldiers joining the mockery. Lightfoot explains the vinegar: "vinegar was the common drink of the Roman soldiers, and hence those to whom the custody of crucified persons was committed had it always ready by them" — the soldiers' own ration, offered in mockery. Gill notes "the Roman soldiers, to whom the execution was committed... joined in the insult, which is not to be wondered at." The witnesses note the soldiers mocking Christ and offering their sour wine in derision.

The witnesses note the soldiers' mockery: offering their vinegar to the crucified King in scorn.

So the witnesses make the verse the soldiers' mockery of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the universal scorn heaped on Christ — Jew and Gentile, ruler and soldier alike — and the patience of the Saviour under it. The witnesses note that "the soldiers also mocked him" — after the people watched and the rulers derided, the Roman soldiers, the executioners, added their mockery; the scorn now coming from the Gentiles as well as the Jews, all classes joining in the derision of the crucified Christ. The expositors observe the vinegar Lightfoot explains: the soldiers offered Christ vinegar — their own common drink, the sour wine that was their ration, kept ready by those guarding the crucified; and they offered it in mockery, jeering at his claim to be a king, perhaps pledging him in scorn as one would a mock-monarch. The witnesses press the universality of the mockery: the rulers mocked his Messiahship, the soldiers his kingship; the Jews and the Romans, the religious and the secular, the leaders and the common soldiers — all united in scorn of the Lord of glory, fulfilling the psalm of the suffering Servant surrounded by his mockers (Psalm 22:6-8). The expositors draw the lesson on the depth of Christ's humiliation: forsaken, condemned, crucified, and now mocked by all — the Creator scorned by his creatures, the King derided by the soldiers, the Saviour jeered as he saved; humiliation heaped on humiliation. The witnesses note the patience of Christ under the universal scorn: he who had prayed for his enemies' forgiveness (v.34) bears the mockery of the soldiers in silence, returning no reviling, the meek Lamb enduring the contempt of all. The expositors observe the providence: even the offering of vinegar fulfilled the Scripture (Psalm 69:21, "in my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink"), the soldiers' mockery accomplishing the prophetic word. The witnesses draw the application: the believer, mocked for Christ, follows a Master mocked by all; and beholds in the universal scorn of the cross the measure of the love that endured it for us. The verse thus records the soldiers' mockery — offering vinegar in scorn — teaching the universal derision heaped on Christ, the fulfilment of prophecy, and the patience of the Saviour under the contempt of all.
37:
"And saying, If thou be the king of the Jews, save thyself." The witnesses note the soldiers' taunt fastening on the kingship. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the soldiers "pick out 'the king of the Jews' as the point of their sneer, the point on which Jesus was condemned"; and presses the irony: "both soldiers and rulers fail to understand that Jesus could not save himself if he was to save others." Gill notes they challenged him, if he were truly their king and Messiah, to deliver himself. The witnesses note the soldiers mocking Christ's royal title, daring him to save himself.

The witnesses note the soldiers' jeer at the King of the Jews — daring the crucified to save himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the soldiers' taunt at Christ's kingship. The lesson the expositors draw is the mockery of the true King, and the irony that he could not save himself if he would save others. The witnesses note the soldiers' jeer: "If thou be the king of the Jews, save thyself" — fastening (Robertson marks) on the kingship, the very charge on which Christ was condemned and which the superscription proclaimed; they mock his royal title, daring him, if he be truly a king, to deliver himself from the cross. The expositors observe the parallel with the rulers' taunt (v.35): as the rulers mocked his Messiahship, the soldiers mock his kingship — both challenging him to save himself, both assuming that his helplessness on the cross disproved his claim. The witnesses press the irony Robertson draws out: both soldiers and rulers failed to understand that Jesus could not save himself if he was to save others — that the saving of the world required precisely that he not come down from the cross; his staying was not weakness but the very work of redemption. The expositors draw the lesson on the true kingship of Christ: the soldiers mocked him as a false king, yet he was the true King — King of the Jews and of all, whose throne was the cross, whose crown was of thorns, whose reign was won not by saving himself but by giving himself; the mockery, like the superscription, unwittingly proclaiming the truth. The witnesses note the blindness of the mockers: they saw only a helpless victim, blind to the King reigning from the tree, accomplishing by his death the salvation of the world; their scorn the measure of their blindness. The expositors observe the patience of Christ: he answers the taunt not by coming down, which would have proved their charge and lost their salvation, but by staying — enduring the mockery to finish the work; the silence and the staying his answer to the jeer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer worships the King who would not save himself that he might save us, and sees in the mocked title the true royalty of the crucified Lord. The verse thus records the soldiers' taunt at Christ's kingship — save thyself — teaching the mockery of the true King, the irony that he could not save himself and others both, and the kingship won not by self-deliverance but by self-giving.
38:
"And a superscription also was written over him in letters of Greek, and Latin, and Hebrew, THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS." The witnesses note the title over the cross and its providential meaning. Theophylact sees the devil's device turned against himself: written in three tongues "that it might not escape one of the passers by, that He was crucified because He made Himself King" — and signifying "that the most powerful of the nations (the Romans), the wisest (the Greeks), those who most worshipped God (the Jews) must be made subject to the dominion of Christ." Ambrose reads it spiritually: "rightly is the title placed above the cross, because Christ's kingdom is not of the human body, but of the power of God... I read 'My kingdom is not of this world.'" The witnesses note the threefold inscription proclaiming Christ King.

The witnesses note the title in three tongues — the mockery overruled to proclaim Christ King to all nations.

So the witnesses make the verse the superscription over the cross. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that turned Pilate's title, meant as a charge or a jeer, into a proclamation of Christ's universal kingship. The witnesses note the inscription: "THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS," written in Greek, Latin, and Hebrew — the three great languages of the world, that all who passed might read it. The expositors press the providence Theophylact marks: the title, intended to state the charge (that Christ was crucified for making himself king), was overruled to publish his kingship to all; and the three tongues signified that the three great peoples — the Romans (the most powerful), the Greeks (the wisest), the Jews (the most religious) — must all be made subject to Christ's dominion; the languages of empire, learning, and religion alike proclaiming him King. The witnesses observe the irony: what Pilate wrote as a charge, and the enemies took as a jeer, God made a herald of the truth — the crucified Christ proclaimed, in the universal tongues, the King not of the Jews only but of all nations. The expositors draw out Ambrose's spiritual reading: the title is rightly placed above the cross, for Christ's kingdom is not of the human body but of the power of God; in the title over the cross we read "My kingdom is not of this world" (John 18:36), and over his head the truth that "the Word was God" (John 1:1) — the cross the throne of a kingdom not earthly but divine. The witnesses note the wonder: at the very moment of his deepest humiliation, Christ is proclaimed King to all the world, the title over the cross the first universal preaching of his kingship. The expositors observe Pilate's unwitting confession: the governor who condemned Christ yet wrote, and would not alter (John 19:21-22), the title of his royalty — the truth proclaimed even by the hand that sentenced him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer reads over the cross the kingship of his Lord, published to all nations, and bows to the King whose throne was the tree. The verse thus records the superscription over the cross — King of the Jews in three tongues — teaching the providence that turned the charge into a proclamation, the universal kingship of Christ, and the divine kingdom proclaimed at the place of his humiliation.
39:
"And one of the malefactors which were hanged railed on him, saying, If thou be Christ, save thyself and us." The witnesses note the impenitent thief's railing and the harmony of the accounts. Gill and Barnes address the apparent contradiction: Matthew and Mark say both thieves reviled him, yet Luke records one penitent — reconciled by supposing both railed at first, the one afterward converted, or that the plural is used loosely. Barnes presses the heart of the railing thief: "this man did not seek for salvation truly; he asked not to be delivered from his sins... men often in affliction call upon God, but it is only to save them from" present suffering, not from sin. The witnesses note the one thief joining the mockers, demanding deliverance from the cross.

The witnesses note the impenitent thief's railing — seeking rescue from the cross, not from his sins.

So the witnesses make the verse the railing of the impenitent thief. The lesson the expositors draw is the hardness of the heart that, even at the gate of death, seeks deliverance from suffering but not from sin. The witnesses note that "one of the malefactors... railed on him" — joining the rulers and soldiers in their mockery, demanding, "If thou be Christ, save thyself and us"; the dying criminal adding his scorn to the chorus, daring Christ to prove himself by escaping the cross. The expositors address the harmony with Matthew and Mark, which say both thieves reviled Christ: the apparent contradiction is reconciled, Gill and Barnes note, by supposing both railed at the first, the one afterward convicted and converted; or that the plural was used in the general way of the abuse heaped on Christ. The witnesses press the heart of the railing thief that Barnes exposes: "save thyself and us" — he sought not salvation from sin, but deliverance from the cross; he wanted rescue from his present suffering, not pardon for his guilt; his cry was the cry of selfish desperation, not of faith. The expositors draw the lesson on false and true seeking: men often, in affliction and the fear of death, call on God — but only to be saved from their pain, not from their sin; and such crying, like the railing thief's, is not faith but self-interest, and finds no answer. The witnesses observe the hardness of the impenitent: even crucified, dying, at the very threshold of eternity, the one thief did not repent, but railed — proof that suffering alone does not soften the heart, and that a man may perish unrepentant in the very presence of the Saviour. The expositors note the contrast about to appear: the same cross, the same Christ, the same nearness to death — yet one thief railed and perished, the other believed and was saved; the difference being not their circumstances but the grace that wrought repentance in the one. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned that nearness to Christ and even the extremity of death do not save the unrepentant; and that true seeking is for deliverance from sin, not merely from suffering. The verse thus records the railing of the impenitent thief — save thyself and us — teaching the hardness that seeks rescue from suffering but not from sin, the harmony of the Gospel accounts, and the warning that even at death's door the heart may perish unrepentant.
40:
"But the other answering rebuked him, saying, Dost not thou fear God, seeing thou art in the same condemnation?" The witnesses note the penitent thief's rebuke and his fear of God. Barnes draws out the reproof: "you are condemned to die as well as he; it is improper for you to rail on him... God is just, and you are hastening to his bar, and you should therefore fear him." Clarke marks the genuine conversion: "the sufferings of this person had been sanctified to him... he is a genuine penitent, and gives the fullest proof he can give of it" — the fear of God now ruling his heart. The witnesses note the one thief, converted, rebuking the other for railing on the innocent Christ.

The witnesses note the penitent thief's rebuke — fearing God where his fellow railed, the first fruit of his conversion.

So the witnesses make the verse the penitent thief's rebuke. The lesson the expositors draw is the evidence of true conversion in the fear of God and the reproof of sin, wrought by grace in a dying criminal. The witnesses note the turn: "the other answering rebuked him" — the one malefactor, now penitent, reproves his fellow for railing on Christ; the first fruit of his conversion a holy indignation at the mockery of the innocent. The expositors press the ground of the rebuke Barnes draws: "Dost not thou fear God, seeing thou art in the same condemnation?" — you are dying as he is, hastening to God's judgment-bar; how dare you rail on this innocent man, when you yourself are about to answer to a just God? The fear of God, which the railing thief had cast off, now rules the penitent's heart. The witnesses observe the marks of genuine conversion Clarke names: the penitent thief's sufferings had been sanctified to him; grace had opened his heart; and he gives, in his few dying words, the fullest proof of true repentance — the fear of God, the rebuke of sin, the confession of his own guilt (the next verse), and faith in Christ (v.42). The expositors draw the lesson on the fear of God as the beginning of conversion: where the impenitent thief railed without fear, the penitent fears God — the first sign of grace, the dawning of the new heart; "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (Proverbs 9:10). The witnesses press the wonder of the work: a dying criminal, in his last hours, on a cross, is converted — his heart turned, his fear awakened, his lips rebuking sin; a marvel of grace, that the Spirit should regenerate a man at the very point of death. The expositors observe the boldness of the new convert: while rulers, soldiers, and his fellow-thief mocked Christ, the penitent alone stood for him, rebuking the railing — confessing Christ before mockers, the courage of true faith. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to mark in the penitent thief the marks of true conversion — the fear of God, the hatred of sin, the confession of guilt, the faith in Christ; and to marvel at the grace that saves even at the last hour. The verse thus records the penitent thief's rebuke — dost thou not fear God — teaching the fear of God as the first fruit of conversion, the evidence of true repentance, and the wonder of grace converting a dying criminal.
41:
"And we indeed justly; for we receive the due reward of our deeds: but this man hath done nothing amiss." The witnesses note the penitent thief's confession of guilt and of Christ's innocence. Gill marks the true sense of sin: "where that is, there will be not only an acknowledgment of the offence, but a vindication of the justice of God." Ellicott notes his confident assertion of Christ's innocence "may have rested on previous knowledge of our Lord's life... or on Pilate's confession." Barnes notes "this man hath done nothing amiss" echoes Christ as the Lamb without blemish (1 Peter 1:19). The witnesses note the penitent owning his just punishment and proclaiming Christ guiltless.

The witnesses note the penitent's twofold confession: his own just guilt and the spotless innocence of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the penitent thief's confession. The lesson the expositors draw is the twofold mark of true repentance — the acknowledgment of one's own just guilt and the recognition of Christ's innocence. The witnesses note the first part: "we indeed justly; for we receive the due reward of our deeds" — the penitent owns his guilt, confesses that his punishment is just, and vindicates the justice of God in condemning him; no excuse, no complaint, but the full acknowledgment of his desert. The expositors press the mark of true repentance Gill names: where there is a real sense of sin, there is not only confession of the offence but a justifying of God — the penitent saying, in effect, that God is righteous though he die, that his punishment is deserved; the broken heart that takes God's side against itself. The witnesses note the second part: "but this man hath done nothing amiss" — the penitent proclaims Christ's innocence, declaring him guiltless even as he confesses his own guilt; the dying thief becoming a witness to the spotless Lamb. The expositors observe the contrast that completes the confession: "we... justly... but this man... nothing amiss" — the penitent sets his own ill desert against Christ's perfect innocence, owning the gulf between the guilty sinner and the sinless Saviour; the very confession that prepares for faith. The witnesses note Ellicott's reflection on the source of his confidence: the thief's assertion of Christ's innocence may have rested on prior knowledge of Jesus's life, on report, or on Pilate's repeated acquittals — however it came, he saw and proclaimed the truth that the rulers denied. The expositors draw the lesson on the wonder of his witness: amid universal mockery, a dying criminal alone bears clear testimony to Christ's innocence and to his own guilt — the two confessions that mark the truly penitent soul. The witnesses press the echo Barnes notes of the Lamb without blemish (1 Peter 1:19): the thief unknowingly confesses the very truth of the atonement — that the innocent dies beside the guilty, the sinless for the sinful. The witnesses draw the application: true repentance owns its own just guilt and Christ's perfect innocence; the believer, like the thief, confesses his desert of judgment and the spotlessness of his Saviour. The verse thus records the penitent's confession — we justly, but this man nothing amiss — teaching the twofold mark of repentance, the witness to Christ's innocence, and the broken heart that justifies God and looks to the sinless Lamb.
42:
"And he said unto Jesus, Lord, remember me when thou comest into thy kingdom." The witnesses note the penitent thief's remarkable faith. Gill marvels at the faith that surpassed the apostles': the thief acknowledged Christ as "Lord," believed in his kingdom "as of a spiritual nature, and not of this world" — "in which respect his faith exceeded that of the apostles themselves, who were looking for a temporal kingdom"; and he believed in Christ's entrance into glory after death and in his second coming. The witnesses note the dying thief praying to be remembered in Christ's kingdom — faith triumphing over the seeming defeat of the cross.

The witnesses note the thief's extraordinary faith: confessing the crucified Christ as King and Lord of a coming kingdom.

So the witnesses make the verse the penitent thief's prayer of faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the extraordinary faith of the dying thief, who saw in the crucified Christ a King and looked to his kingdom beyond death. The witnesses note the prayer: "Lord, remember me when thou comest into thy kingdom" — the thief addresses Christ as "Lord," owns him as King, and asks only to be remembered when he comes into his kingdom; a humble, believing plea for a place in the mercy of the King. The expositors press the wonder of this faith that Gill exalts: the thief believed, against all appearance, that the crucified man beside him was a King with a kingdom — when Christ hung dying, mocked, forsaken, with no sign of royalty but the cross and the thorns; faith piercing through the deepest humiliation to behold the King. The witnesses observe how his faith surpassed the apostles' in several respects: he believed the kingdom was spiritual, not of this world — where the apostles still looked for an earthly kingdom; he believed Christ would enter his glory through death — when the apostles were scattered in despair; and he looked beyond to Christ's second coming, asking to be remembered then. The expositors draw the lesson on the triumph of faith: at the very hour when faith seemed most impossible — Christ crucified, the disciples fled — a dying thief believed, confessing the King in the crucified; the brightest faith shining in the darkest hour. The witnesses note the humility of the prayer: he asks not for a throne, nor for deliverance from the cross, but only to be "remembered" — content with the least place in the King's mercy; the lowliness of true faith. The expositors observe the work of grace: such faith was no work of nature, but the gift of the Spirit, who enlightened the thief's mind and wrought belief in his heart — a marvel of sovereign grace, converting and giving faith at the point of death. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to marvel at and imitate the thief's faith — owning the crucified Christ as King, looking beyond death to his kingdom, and asking humbly to be remembered; the faith that lays hold of Christ even in the darkest hour. The verse thus records the penitent thief's prayer of faith — Lord, remember me — teaching the extraordinary faith that saw the King in the crucified, surpassing the apostles', and the humble plea that laid hold of mercy at the point of death.
43:
"And Jesus said unto him, Verily I say unto thee, To day shalt thou be with me in paradise." The witnesses note Christ's gracious answer and the meaning of paradise. Gill notes Christ "answered him, though he said not one word to the other that railed... and promised him more than he asked for, and sooner than he expected." Lightfoot examines "paradise" — "in common Jewish speech, the garden of Eden," the place of the blessed dead. The witnesses note the immediacy — "to day" — the soul of the believer with Christ at death, in the place of blessedness; the penitent thief assured not merely of remembrance at the last day, but of present blessedness that very day.

The witnesses note Christ's answer: that very day the penitent would be with him in paradise — present blessedness at death.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's promise to the penitent thief. The lesson the expositors draw is the riches of Christ's grace, granting more than was asked and sooner than expected, and the comfort of the soul's immediate presence with Christ at death. The witnesses note the immediate answer: while Christ was silent to the railing thief and the mockers, he at once answered the penitent — and granted, Gill notes, more than he asked and sooner than he hoped; the thief had asked to be remembered when Christ came into his kingdom (in the future), but Christ promises him present, immediate blessedness, "to day." The expositors press the force of "to day": not at some distant coming, but this very day — before the sun set, the penitent would be with Christ in paradise; the immediacy of the gift overwhelming the thief's humble request. The witnesses note the meaning of "paradise" that Lightfoot explains: in Jewish speech, paradise was the garden of Eden, the abode of the blessed dead, the place of rest and joy in the presence of God; Christ promises the thief a place there, with himself. The expositors draw the great doctrine: "to day shalt thou be with me" — the soul of the believer, at death, passes immediately into the conscious presence of Christ; not into sleep or unconsciousness, nor into a long waiting, but at once into paradise with the Lord (cf. Philippians 1:23, "to depart and to be with Christ"; 2 Corinthians 5:8). The witnesses press the comfort: the penitent thief, that very day, would be with Christ in bliss — and so every believer, at death, is with the Lord; the body laid in the grave, the soul received into paradise. The expositors observe the riches of grace displayed: a dying thief, converted in his last hours, is assured of immediate glory — the freeness and the power of grace, that saves to the uttermost and at the latest hour, granting paradise to one who, but moments before, was a railing criminal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is comforted that death ushers the soul at once into Christ's presence; and is taught the freeness of grace, that saved the thief at the last and assured him of paradise that very day. The verse thus records Christ's promise to the penitent thief — to day in paradise — teaching the riches of grace granting more than was asked, the soul's immediate presence with Christ at death, and the comfort and freeness of so great a salvation.
44:
"And it was about the sixth hour, and there was a darkness over all the earth until the ninth hour." The witnesses note the supernatural darkness at the crucifixion. Cyril reads it as creation mourning: "the whole framework of the world bewailed its rightful Master, and the light was darkened at mid-day, a manifest token that the souls of those who crucified Him would suffer darkness." Augustine presses that it was no natural eclipse: "this darkening of the sun did not happen in the regular course of the heavenly bodies, because it was then the Passover, always at the full moon; but a regular eclipse takes place only at new moon." Dionysius reports the marvel observed at Heliopolis. The witnesses note the three hours' darkness over the land — a portent, not a natural eclipse.

The witnesses note the supernatural darkness — creation mourning its Maker, no natural eclipse but a portent of judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the supernatural darkness at the crucifixion. The lesson the expositors draw is that the darkness was a divine portent — creation mourning its crucified Maker and a sign of judgment — and could be no natural eclipse. The witnesses note the time and extent: "about the sixth hour" (noon) "there was a darkness over all the earth until the ninth hour" (three in the afternoon) — three hours of darkness at the very height of day, when the sun should have shone brightest. The expositors press Augustine's demonstration that this was no natural eclipse: it was the Passover, always held at the full moon, when the moon is opposite the sun; but a solar eclipse can occur only at the new moon, when the moon is between earth and sun; therefore the darkness at the Passover full moon was contrary to nature, a supernatural sign, not an astronomical event. The witnesses observe the marvel Dionysius reports — the unnatural obscuration witnessed even at Heliopolis in Egypt, the moon seen to meet the sun out of its course — a wonder noted beyond Judea. The expositors draw out Cyril's reading of the meaning: the darkness was creation mourning its rightful Master, the framework of the world bewailing the death of its Maker; and a token of judgment, that the souls of those who crucified the Lord of light would suffer darkness — the withdrawal of the natural light a sign of the spiritual darkness falling on the guilty nation. The witnesses press the fitness of the portent: when the Sun of righteousness was being extinguished (in appearance) on the cross, the sun of nature was darkened; the heavens themselves veiling their face at the death of the Lord, refusing to shine on the murder of the Son of God. The expositors observe the awe of the scene: three hours of midday darkness over the land, the world hushed in unnatural night while the Saviour hung dying — a sign to all that no common death was being accomplished, but the death of the Lord of creation. The witnesses draw the application: the darkness proclaims the dignity of the Crucified and the judgment on his murderers; the believer beholds in it creation's mourning and a warning of the darkness that falls on those who reject the light. The verse thus records the supernatural darkness — three hours at midday — teaching that it was no natural eclipse but a divine portent, creation mourning its Maker, and a sign of judgment on those who crucified the Lord of light.
45:
"And the sun was darkened, and the veil of the temple was rent in the midst." The witnesses note the darkened sun and the rending of the veil. The grammatical-historical Robertson discusses the reading "the sun's light failing" (used also of an eclipse), and the textual variant "the sun was darkened" adopted to avoid the difficulty (since an eclipse was impossible at the full moon). Gill notes the eclipse "was preternatural" and total. On the veil, the witnesses note its rending "in the midst" — the great curtain before the Holy of Holies torn from top to bottom (Matthew 27:51), opening the way into the holiest. The witnesses note the two signs: the sun darkened and the temple veil rent.

The witnesses note the darkened sun and the rent veil — the way into the holiest opened by Christ's death.

So the witnesses make the verse the darkened sun and the rending of the veil. The lesson the expositors draw is the meaning of the two signs — the darkness a portent of judgment, the rent veil the opening of the way to God by Christ's death. The witnesses note the darkened sun: Robertson discusses the reading "the sun's light failing," and the textual care taken (since a natural eclipse was impossible at the full moon, some copies read "the sun was darkened" to avoid the technical term for eclipse); but all agree the darkness was preternatural, total, a supernatural sign. The expositors turn to the greater sign: "the veil of the temple was rent in the midst" — the heavy curtain that hung before the Holy of Holies, barring all but the high priest, and him only once a year, was torn (Matthew 27:51 adds, from top to bottom) at the moment of Christ's death. The witnesses press the meaning of the rent veil: it signified that the way into the holiest, into the immediate presence of God, was now opened by the death of Christ — the barrier removed, the access granted; "a new and living way, which he hath consecrated for us, through the veil, that is to say, his flesh" (Hebrews 10:19-20). The expositors draw the lesson on the abolition of the old order: the rending of the veil declared the end of the temple service, the ceremonial law fulfilled and set aside; the typical worship, with its barriers and its one yearly entrance, giving way to the open access purchased by Christ's blood. The witnesses observe the timing: the veil was rent at the very moment of Christ's death, by an unseen hand, from top to bottom — God himself opening what man had kept closed, declaring that the sacrifice was accepted and the way to him now free. The expositors note the contrast of the two signs: the darkness a sign of judgment on the guilty, the rent veil a sign of grace to the believing — the same death that condemns the rejecters opens the way for the penitent. The witnesses draw the application: the believer enters with boldness into the holiest by the blood of Jesus (Hebrews 10:19), the veil rent, the access free; and beholds in the torn curtain the great purchase of his death. The verse thus records the darkened sun and the rent veil — teaching the portent of judgment and, above all, the opening of the way to God by Christ's death, the end of the old order, and the free access into the holiest now granted to all who believe.
46:
"And when Jesus had cried with a loud voice, he said, Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit: and having said thus, he gave up the ghost." The witnesses note Christ's last word and his voluntary death. Clarke draws the doctrine: "into thy hands I commit my spirit... another proof of the immateriality of the soul, and of its separate existence when the body is dead" (Psalm 31:5; 1 Peter 2:23). Ellicott notes the rendering "he expired, or breathed out His spirit," and that the loud cry immediately preceded death. The witnesses note Christ committing his spirit to the Father and breathing out his life — the loud voice a sign of his unspent strength, the death a willing surrender.

The witnesses note Christ's last word — committing his spirit to the Father — and his voluntary surrender of life.

So the witnesses make the verse the death of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the willing, trustful surrender of Christ's spirit to the Father, and the voluntariness of his death. The witnesses note the last word: "Father, into thy hands I commend my spirit" — Christ, dying, commits his spirit to the Father, entrusting his soul to God in perfect trust and peace; the words of Psalm 31:5, taken on his lips as he died. The expositors press the doctrine Clarke draws: "I commend my spirit" proves the immateriality of the soul and its separate existence when the body dies — Christ's spirit, distinct from his body, commended to the Father even as his body was given to death; the soul living on, received by God, while the flesh died. The witnesses observe the trustful surrender: Christ dies not in fear or despair, but in confidence, committing his spirit to his Father's hands — the pattern for every believer's death, to yield the soul into the keeping of God (as Stephen, Acts 7:59). The expositors draw the lesson on the voluntariness of his death, marked by the loud cry: Christ "cried with a loud voice" — a cry of strength, not the faint expiring of an exhausted man; and then, having said his word, "gave up the ghost" — Ellicott notes the sense, "breathed out his spirit"; he died, not because his strength was spent, but because he willingly surrendered his life, laying it down of himself (John 10:18). The witnesses press the wonder the centurion would mark (the next verse): that Christ, after a loud cry, immediately died — showing what power he had even in dying, the death a voluntary act, not a mere succumbing. The expositors observe the peace and dignity of his end: no struggle, no despair, but a trustful word and a willing surrender — the Son commending his spirit to the Father, then breathing it out; the death of the Lord of life, freely given for the world. The witnesses draw the application: the believer learns from Christ's death to commit his spirit to God in trust, and beholds in the loud cry and willing surrender the truth that Christ laid down his life of himself, no man taking it from him. The verse thus records the death of Christ — Father, into thy hands — teaching the trustful surrender of his spirit to the Father, the immortality of the soul, and the voluntariness of the death by which he laid down his life for us.
47:
"Now when the centurion saw what was done, he glorified God, saying, Certainly this was a righteous man." The witnesses note the centurion's confession and the harmony with the other Gospels. Augustine reconciles the accounts: where Matthew has the centurion marvel "at the earthquake and the things done," Luke says he marvelled "that Jesus while uttering the loud voice expired, showing what power He had when dying"; Luke's "what was done" includes all the marvels as one wondrous event. And where Matthew and Mark give "Truly this was the Son of God," Luke gives "a righteous man" — the centurion confessing both. The witnesses note the Gentile soldier, moved by Christ's death and the signs, glorifying God and confessing his innocence.

The witnesses note the centurion's confession: a Gentile soldier glorifying God and owning Christ righteous.

So the witnesses make the verse the centurion's confession. The lesson the expositors draw is the witness of the Gentile soldier to Christ's innocence and divinity, and the harmony of the Gospel accounts. The witnesses note that "the centurion saw what was done" — the manner of Christ's death, the loud cry, the immediate expiring, the darkness, the earthquake (Matthew 27:54) — and, moved by it all, "glorified God, saying, Certainly this was a righteous man." The expositors observe the harmony Augustine works out: Matthew and Mark give the centurion's confession as "Truly this was the Son of God," Luke as "a righteous man"; not a contradiction, but the fuller report of what the centurion said — confessing both Christ's innocence and his divine sonship; Luke records the one, Matthew and Mark the other, the centurion uttering both. The witnesses press the wonder of the confession: a Roman centurion, a Gentile, the officer charged with the execution, is the one who confesses Christ — where the rulers mocked and the people watched, the heathen soldier glorifies God and owns the Crucified righteous and the Son of God; the first fruits of the Gentiles, confessing at the cross what Israel denied. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of Christ's death: the very manner of his dying — the majestic loud cry, the willing surrender, the attendant signs — convinced the centurion that this was no common man, no guilty criminal, but a righteous man, the Son of God; the cross itself a sermon that converted the executioner. The witnesses note the irony and the grace: the rulers, with all their Scriptures, rejected Christ; the Gentile soldier, with the evidence of his eyes, confessed him — a foreshadowing of the gospel passing to the Gentiles, who would believe where Israel refused. The expositors observe that the centurion "glorified God" — his confession not mere admiration but worship, giving glory to God in owning Christ; the beginning, perhaps, of saving faith in the soldier's heart. The witnesses draw the application: the death of Christ, rightly beheld, draws the confession of his righteousness and divinity even from a Gentile soldier; and the believer joins the centurion in glorifying God and owning the Crucified as the righteous Son of God. The verse thus records the centurion's confession — certainly a righteous man — teaching the witness of the Gentile to Christ's innocence and divinity, the harmony of the Gospels, and the converting power of the cross.
48:
"And all the people that came together to that sight, beholding the things which were done, smote their breasts, and returned." The witnesses note the people's stricken remorse. Barnes explains: they "smote their breasts in token of alarm, fear, and anguish; they saw the judgments of God; they saw the guilt of the rulers; and they feared the further displeasure of the Almighty." Clarke notes "all were deeply affected except the priests... the darkness, earthquake, etc., had brought terror into every heart." Ellicott marks the word for "sight" as expressing the spectacle they had come to watch. The witnesses note the crowd, who came to watch, returning home smiting their breasts in remorse.

The witnesses note the people's remorse: the crowd that came to mock returning home smiting their breasts.

So the witnesses make the verse the people's stricken remorse. The lesson the expositors draw is the change wrought in the crowd by the signs of Christ's death — from spectacle-seekers to smitten consciences. The witnesses note the turn: "all the people that came together to that sight" — the crowd that had gathered to watch the crucifixion as a spectacle (Ellicott marks the word) — now, "beholding the things which were done" (the darkness, the manner of Christ's death, the signs), "smote their breasts, and returned." The expositors press the meaning of the breast-smiting that Barnes explains: a token of alarm, fear, and anguish — the people, seeing the judgments of God in the darkness and the signs, struck their breasts in remorse and dread; convicted of the guilt in which they had shared, fearing the further wrath of the Almighty. The witnesses observe the change Clarke notes: all were deeply affected, save the hardened priests — the terror of the darkness and the earthquake bringing fear into every heart; the crowd that had come to watch, perhaps to mock, going home stricken with conscience. The expositors draw the lesson on the power of the cross to convict: the signs attending Christ's death awoke the consciences of the people, turning their idle curiosity into remorse; the spectacle they came to enjoy became the occasion of their fear, the death of Christ pressing on their hearts. The witnesses note the limited and mingled nature of this remorse: it was not yet repentance unto salvation, but a stricken conscience, a fear of judgment — yet a beginning, in many, that would ripen at Pentecost into true repentance, when the same people, "pricked in their heart" (Acts 2:37), would believe. The expositors observe the contrast with the priests: the people were moved, but the rulers were hardened; the signs that smote the crowd left the leaders unrepentant — the difference between the convicted and the obdurate. The witnesses draw the application: the death of Christ, with its attendant signs, smites the conscience; and the believer is to mark how the cross convicts even the careless crowd, and to pray that conviction ripen into saving repentance. The verse thus records the people's stricken remorse — smiting their breasts — teaching the convicting power of the cross, the change from spectacle to remorse, and the beginning of the conviction that would ripen into repentance.
49:
"And all his acquaintance, and the women that followed him from Galilee, stood afar off, beholding these things." The witnesses note the faithful watchers at a distance. Ellicott notes "acquaintance" is used only here, "intermediate between the spectators and the avowed disciples" — perhaps such as Lazarus, or secret believers, or those who had served him. On the women, he notes Luke does not name them, having already named the chief in Luke 8:2-3. Barnes cross-references the psalm of the forsaken sufferer (Psalm 38:11). The witnesses note Christ's friends and the faithful women standing at a distance, beholding his death.

The witnesses note the faithful — friends and the women from Galilee — watching the death of Christ from afar.

So the witnesses make the verse the faithful watchers at the cross. The lesson the expositors draw is the devotion of the friends and women who, though at a distance, stood faithful to the end when others had fled. The witnesses note who watched: "all his acquaintance, and the women that followed him from Galilee, stood afar off, beholding these things." The expositors observe Ellicott's note on "acquaintance" — a word used only here, marking a group intermediate between the casual spectators and the avowed disciples; perhaps secret believers, or those who had known and served Christ — who, with the women, kept their place near the cross. The witnesses press the faithfulness of the women especially: they "followed him from Galilee," had ministered to him (Luke 8:2-3), and now stood by his cross when the apostles (save John) had fled — the devotion of the women conspicuous in the hour of desertion; last at the cross, as they would be first at the tomb. The expositors draw the lesson on the love that endures: when the disciples scattered, these friends and women remained, watching the death of their Lord; their love, though it stood "afar off" (perhaps from fear, perhaps kept back by the soldiers), did not forsake him, but beheld his sufferings to the end. The witnesses note the poignancy Barnes marks from the psalm: "my lovers and my friends stand aloof from my sore; and my kinsmen stand afar off" (Psalm 38:11) — the forsaken sufferer's friends at a distance, fulfilled in the watchers who stood afar; yet their standing afar was not desertion but faithful, sorrowing witness. The expositors observe the honour Luke gives the faithful women throughout his Gospel: here again they are remembered, the constant ministers and witnesses, present at the cross, the burial, and the resurrection — the steadfast love that the evangelist of compassion records. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to be faithful to Christ to the end, as these were, not forsaking him in the dark hour; and to mark the devotion of the women, whose love outlasted the apostles' courage. The verse thus records the faithful watchers at the cross — friends and the women from Galilee, beholding from afar — teaching the devotion that endured when others fled, the faithfulness of the women, and the love that watched the death of the Lord.
50:
"And, behold, there was a man named Joseph, a counsellor; and he was a good man, and a just:" The witnesses note Joseph of Arimathea, the honourable disciple. The Greek Expositor notes Joseph "had been at one time a secret disciple, but at length, bursting through the bonds of fear and becoming very zealous, he took down the body of our Lord." Bede explains "counsellor" (decurio): "of the order of the council... of high rank in the world, but of still higher estimation before God; a good man and a just" — fitted "by the righteousness of his works for the burial of our Lord's body." The witnesses note Joseph, a member of the council, good and just, coming forward to bury Christ.

The witnesses note Joseph of Arimathea — a councillor, good and just, who broke through fear to honour Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction of Joseph of Arimathea. The lesson the expositors draw is the courage and character of the honourable disciple, who at the last broke through his fear to render Christ the last offices of love. The witnesses note the man: "Joseph, a counsellor" — a member of the Sanhedrin, the very council that condemned Christ — "a good man, and a just." The expositors observe Bede's note on his rank: a "decurio," of the order of the council, of high standing in the world; yet, more importantly, of high estimation before God, good and just — fitted by the righteousness of his life for the honour of burying the Lord. The witnesses press the Greek Expositor's account of his courage: Joseph had been a secret disciple, holding back for fear (John 19:38); but now, at the death of Christ, when the bravest had fled, he burst through the bonds of fear and became zealous, coming forward boldly to claim and bury the body — his timid faith ripening, in the dark hour, into open courage. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence and the grace: that one of the council itself, a good and just man who had not consented to their deed (the next verse), should be the one to bury Christ — God raising up a friend in the very camp of his enemies, and giving Joseph courage at the last to confess the Lord he had secretly followed. The witnesses note the honour of his character: "a good man, and a just" — not merely of rank, but of righteousness; the qualities that fitted him to handle the body of the holy One, and that mark him as a true, if hitherto secret, disciple. The expositors observe the timing of his courage: when Christ was dead, when there seemed nothing to gain and much to lose, Joseph came forward — faith showing itself boldest when the cause seemed lost; the secret disciple openly confessing the crucified. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to learn from Joseph to break through fear and confess Christ, especially when it is costly; and to mark how grace ripens timid faith into courage. The verse thus introduces Joseph of Arimathea — a councillor, good and just — teaching the courage that broke through fear, the character that fitted him to bury the Lord, and the providence that raised up a friend among the council to honour the body of Christ.
51:
"(The same had not consented to the counsel and deed of them;) he was of Arimathaea, a city of the Jews: who also himself waited for the kingdom of God." The witnesses note Joseph's dissent and his hope. Gill notes that though Joseph "was with the Jews... being one of that great council, yet he did not agree with them, nor was it his advice that they should put Christ to death." Ellicott distinguishes "the counsel and deed": the "counsel" all the council's action from Caiaphas's advice to the condemnation, the "deed" the unofficial acts (the compact with Judas, the delivery to Pilate); and notes Joseph "waited for the kingdom of God." The witnesses note Joseph dissenting from the council's crime and looking for God's kingdom.

The witnesses note Joseph's dissent from the council and his waiting for the kingdom of God.

So the witnesses make the verse Joseph's dissent and his expectation. The lesson the expositors draw is the integrity of Joseph, who had no part in the council's crime, and the hope that marked him as a true disciple. The witnesses note the first point: "the same had not consented to the counsel and deed of them" — Joseph, though a member of the Sanhedrin, had not agreed to their condemnation of Christ nor to the deeds that compassed his death; Gill notes he did not give his advice or consent to the murder. The expositors observe Ellicott's distinction: "the counsel" embraces all the council's official action against Christ, from Caiaphas's advice to the final sentence; "the deed," the unofficial acts, the bargain with Judas, the delivery to Pilate — and from all of it Joseph stood apart, keeping his hands clean of innocent blood.
