The witnesses press the integrity this shows: in a body resolved on Christ's death, Joseph dissented — whether by voice or by absence, he would not share the guilt; a righteous man who would not be carried along by the majority into the murder of the innocent. The expositors draw the lesson on standing apart from sin: the believer, even when surrounded by those resolved on evil, is to keep himself pure, refusing consent to the wickedness of the many; Joseph's dissent the mark of a conscience not overborne by the crowd. The witnesses note the second point: Joseph "waited for the kingdom of God" — he looked, in faith and hope, for the coming of the Messiah's kingdom; a true Israelite, expecting the redemption of God, which marked him (with Simeon and Anna, Luke 2:25, 38) as one of the faithful remnant. The expositors observe that this waiting was the root of his discipleship: looking for the kingdom, he had received Christ (secretly) as the King; and now, at his death, came forward to honour him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to keep clear of the sins of his society, as Joseph did, and to wait for the kingdom of God — the hope that marks the true disciple. The verse thus records Joseph's dissent and his expectation — not consenting to the deed, waiting for the kingdom — teaching the integrity that stood apart from the council's crime, and the hope of the kingdom that marked him a true disciple.
52:
"This man went unto Pilate, and begged the body of Jesus." The witnesses note Joseph's bold request. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "the middle voice shows that Joseph asked the body of Jesus as a personal favour"; and that Mark says he went "boldly" to Pilate (Mark 15:43). Gill notes the same boldness. The witnesses note Joseph, breaking through fear, going boldly to Pilate to ask for Christ's body — a public act of devotion at great personal risk.

The witnesses note Joseph's bold petition: going to Pilate to beg the body of Jesus.

So the witnesses make the verse Joseph's bold request for the body. The lesson the expositors draw is the courage and devotion of Joseph, who at great risk came forward to claim and honour the body of the crucified Christ. The witnesses note the act: "this man went unto Pilate, and begged the body of Jesus" — Joseph, the secret disciple, now openly approached the Roman governor to ask for Christ's body, that he might bury it. The expositors press the boldness Mark marks (15:43) and Robertson notes: Joseph "went in boldly" to Pilate — a courageous act, for to claim the body of one crucified as a rebel was to confess himself a follower of the condemned, exposing himself to the hatred of the council and the suspicion of Rome; yet he did it openly, his fear now conquered by love. The witnesses observe Robertson's note on the middle voice: Joseph "asked the body... as a personal favour" — begging it for himself, a personal and earnest petition, that he might render Christ the last offices of love. The expositors draw the lesson on the courage of love: when the apostles had fled and the cause seemed utterly lost, Joseph came forward — his devotion showing itself boldest in the darkest hour; the timid disciple becoming brave, the secret follower confessing openly. The witnesses note the providence in this: that the body of Christ should be claimed and honourably buried, not left to the indignity of a criminal's disposal nor cast into a common grave; God moving Joseph (and Nicodemus) to secure an honourable burial, fulfilling the Scripture that he should be "with the rich in his death" (Isaiah 53:9). The expositors observe the contrast with Joseph's former timidity: once a secret disciple "for fear of the Jews" (John 19:38), he now braves all to honour Christ — the grace that ripens fearful faith into bold confession. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to learn from Joseph to confess Christ boldly, especially when it is costly and the cause seems lost; and to render the Lord the offices of love without fear. The verse thus records Joseph's bold request — begging the body of Jesus — teaching the courage and devotion that braved all to honour Christ, the providence securing an honourable burial, and the grace that turned fear into bold confession.
53:
"And he took it down, and wrapped it in linen, and laid it in a sepulchre that was hewn in stone, wherein never man before was laid." The witnesses note the honourable burial of Christ. Bede draws the spiritual lesson from "the simple burial of our Lord," and the fitness of the new tomb. Lightfoot supplies Jewish background on the wrapping and the treatment of those executed. The witnesses note the care of the burial — the body taken down, wrapped in clean linen, laid in a new, unused tomb hewn in rock — an honourable interment fulfilling prophecy (Isaiah 53:9). The witnesses note Christ buried with reverence in a new sepulchre.

The witnesses note the honourable burial: Christ wrapped in linen and laid in a new, unused tomb.

So the witnesses make the verse the honourable burial of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the reverent care of the burial, its fulfilment of prophecy, and the significance of the new tomb. The witnesses note the acts: Joseph "took it down" from the cross, "wrapped it in linen" — clean, fine linen, with the spices Nicodemus brought (John 19:39-40) — "and laid it in a sepulchre that was hewn in stone, wherein never man before was laid." The expositors press the honour of the burial: the body of Christ, which his enemies would have cast into a criminal's grave, was tenderly handled, wrapped in fine linen, and laid in a costly rock-hewn tomb — an honourable interment fulfilling Isaiah's word that he should be "with the rich in his death" (Isaiah 53:9), the rich man's tomb receiving the crucified. The witnesses observe the significance of the new tomb that Bede and the expositors mark: "wherein never man before was laid" — a fresh, unused sepulchre, that there might be no question, when he rose, whose body had risen; no other had lain there, so the resurrection could not be confused with another; and the purity of the new tomb befitting the holy body of the Lord. The expositors draw the spiritual lessons: as Christ was laid in a virgin tomb, so he had been born of a virgin womb — the unused tomb answering the unused womb, the purity of his entrance into the world matched by the purity of his resting-place in death; and the new tomb a token that in him all things are made new. The witnesses note the providence in the details: the wrapping, the spices, the rock-hewn tomb sealed with a stone — all serving to confirm the reality of his death and the certainty of his resurrection; the careful burial laying the ground for the empty tomb. The expositors observe the love of Joseph and Nicodemus: rich men, members of the council, lavishing on the dead Christ the honours of an costly burial — their devotion rendering to the Lord, in death, the reverence his enemies denied him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer beholds in the honourable burial the fulfilment of prophecy and the dignity of the Lord even in death; and is assured, by the new and witnessed tomb, of the resurrection to come. The verse thus records the honourable burial of Christ — wrapped in linen, laid in a new tomb — teaching the reverent care of the burial, the fulfilment of prophecy, and the significance of the virgin tomb that confirmed the resurrection.
54:
"And that day was the preparation, and the sabbath drew on." The witnesses note the timing of the burial before the sabbath. Gill notes "that day was the preparation, both for the sabbath, and for the Chagigah, or grand festival." Lightfoot discusses the phrase "the sabbath drew on" (literally, "began to dawn") and the Jewish reckoning of the sabbath's approach with the lighting of the sabbath candles at evening. The witnesses note the burial done in haste on the preparation day, as the sabbath was about to begin.

The witnesses note the timing: the burial hurried before the approaching sabbath.

So the witnesses make the verse the timing of the burial. The lesson the expositors draw is the haste of the burial before the sabbath, and the providential ordering of the times. The witnesses note that "that day was the preparation" — the Friday, the day of preparation for the sabbath (and, Gill adds, for the great festival of the feast); and "the sabbath drew on" — the sabbath was about to begin at sunset, so the burial must be completed quickly, before the holy day. The expositors observe Lightfoot's note on the phrase "drew on" (literally "began to dawn") — the Jewish reckoning of the sabbath's approach, marked by the lighting of the sabbath candles at evening; for the Jewish day began at sundown, so the sabbath "dawned" at evening. The witnesses press the haste this imposed: the burial had to be done before the sabbath began, leaving no time for the full anointing and preparation of the body that the women desired (the next verses); hence the body was wrapped and laid in the tomb quickly, and the women planned to complete the offices after the sabbath. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence in the timing: Christ died and was buried on the preparation day, rested in the tomb over the sabbath, and rose on the first day — the very pattern foretold and fitting, his body resting in the tomb on the sabbath of the old creation, and rising to begin the new. The witnesses note the fulfilment of the sign of Jonah: Christ would be "three days and three nights" (in the Jewish reckoning of parts of days) in the heart of the earth (Matthew 12:40) — the burial on Friday, the sabbath rest, the resurrection on Sunday. The expositors observe the reverence even in haste: though the burial was hurried by the approaching sabbath, it was honourable and complete enough, the women purposing to add their spices after the holy day. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that ordered the times of Christ's death, burial, and resurrection to fulfil the Scriptures and the appointed pattern. The verse thus records the timing of the burial — the preparation day, the sabbath drawing on — teaching the haste imposed by the approaching sabbath, the Jewish reckoning of the day, and the providence ordering the times of Christ's burial and rest.
55:
"And the women also, which came with him from Galilee, followed after, and beheld the sepulchre, and how his body was laid." The witnesses note the faithful women marking the tomb. Ellicott sees here "traces of St. Luke's informants" — the women who came from Galilee, who "stood within view of the cross, saw the body taken down, and followed to the garden"; their careful observation the source of Luke's detail. Clarke names them: Mary Magdalene, Joanna, and Mary the mother of James (Luke 24:10). The witnesses note the women following to the tomb and observing exactly where and how Christ was laid.

The witnesses note the faithful women marking the tomb — observing exactly where and how Christ was laid.

So the witnesses make the verse the women marking the tomb. The lesson the expositors draw is the faithful devotion of the women, whose careful observation prepared for their part at the resurrection. The witnesses note that "the women... which came with him from Galilee, followed after, and beheld the sepulchre, and how his body was laid" — the faithful women, who had stood by the cross (v.49), now followed the body to the tomb, and carefully marked the place and the manner of the burial. The expositors observe Ellicott's note that here are traces of Luke's informants: the women's careful observation — where the tomb was, how the body was laid — is the very detail Luke records, gathered from these eyewitnesses; their devotion the source of the Gospel's account. The witnesses press the faithfulness this shows: when the apostles had fled, the women remained — at the cross, at the burial, and (soon) at the tomb; their love steadfast through every dark hour, last to leave and first to return. The expositors draw the lesson on the purpose of their observation: the women marked the tomb that they might return after the sabbath to complete the burial with their spices (the next verse); their careful noting of the place ensured they would come to the right tomb — the very tomb they would find empty on the resurrection morning. The witnesses note the providence in this: the women's exact observation made them sure witnesses both of the burial and of the empty tomb — they knew where Christ was laid, so that their finding it empty could not be mistaken; their devotion serving the evidence of the resurrection. The expositors observe the honour Luke gives the women: throughout the Passion and resurrection, the faithful women are the constant witnesses — at the cross, the tomb, and the rising; their love and their testimony woven through the Gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to imitate the women's faithful devotion, following Christ to the end, marking and treasuring the things concerning him; and to value their witness, on which the certainty of the burial and resurrection in part rests. The verse thus records the women marking the tomb — beholding how his body was laid — teaching the faithful devotion of the women, their careful observation that prepared for the resurrection witness, and the love that followed Christ even to the grave.
56:
"And they returned, and prepared spices and ointments; and rested the sabbath day according to the commandment." The witnesses note the women's loving preparation and their sabbath rest. Lightfoot discusses the timing — the women had the interval before the sabbath to buy spices, and the Jewish reckoning of the sabbath's onset "between the suns." Gill notes they "rested the sabbath day according to the commandment." The witnesses note the women preparing spices to anoint Christ's body, then keeping the sabbath rest before returning on the first day. The witnesses note the union of love and obedience in the faithful women.

The witnesses note the women's love and obedience: preparing spices for Christ, yet resting the sabbath as commanded.

So the witnesses make the verse the women's preparation and sabbath rest. The lesson the expositors draw is the beautiful union of love and obedience in the faithful women, who would honour Christ's body yet keep God's commandment. The witnesses note the two acts: the women "prepared spices and ointments" — purchasing and making ready the materials to anoint Christ's body, a labour of love, that they might give him a fuller, more honourable burial than the haste before the sabbath had allowed; and they "rested the sabbath day according to the commandment" — keeping the sabbath rest, even in their grief and their eagerness to anoint their Lord. The expositors press Lightfoot's note on the timing: the women had the brief interval before the sabbath began to buy and prepare the spices, completing their purchases before the holy day's onset ("between the suns"); then they rested, deferring the anointing until the sabbath was past. The witnesses observe the union of love and obedience: their love would have them anoint Christ at once, but their obedience kept them from work on the sabbath; they honoured the commandment even while their hearts longed to honour their Lord — love and duty in perfect harmony. The expositors draw the lesson on sabbath-keeping: the women, devoted as they were, would not break the sabbath even to anoint Christ's body; their reverence for God's commandment governing even their devotion — a rebuke to those who would set aside God's law for the sake of zeal. The witnesses note the providence in their rest: by resting the sabbath, the women came to the tomb on the first day (Luke 24:1) — and so were the first witnesses of the resurrection; their obedience bringing them to the empty tomb on the morning of the rising. The expositors observe the poignancy: the women prepared spices to anoint a dead body, not knowing he would rise; their love planning for a burial that the resurrection would render unnecessary — yet their devotion honoured and rewarded with the first sight of the risen Lord. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to unite love and obedience as the women did — zealous to honour Christ, yet reverent of God's commandments; and to mark how their faithful rest brought them to the joy of the resurrection. The verse thus records the women's preparation and sabbath rest — spices made ready, the sabbath kept — teaching the union of love and obedience, the reverence for God's commandment, and the faithful devotion that would bring them, after the rest, to the empty tomb.
